string.text: Those who have been walking the Zone for a long time, as far as I know, are interested in something beyond money, booze, and wish-fulfillers. Maybe I'm wrong, but I think I heard in the bar that among you there’s a trend for philosophical sayings. Care to tell an interesting parable?
Thank you.
Thanks.

Well, how can I put it… there’s a trend among experienced stalkers. We take turns getting hooked on one thing or another. Listen. They say that once, King Mahmud outpaced his retinue. Riding at full speed along the river, he suddenly saw a small boy near the water, fishing with a net. The boy seemed very unhappy. The king sharply reined in his horse, approached the boy, and asked: — My child, why do you look so sad? I have never met a person more sorrowful than you. The boy replied: — Your Majesty, we are seven brothers. Our father has died, and we live in extreme poverty with our mother. To feed ourselves, I come to the river every day and cast my net. If I fail to catch a single fish during the day, I go to bed hungry. — My son, — said the king, — if you don’t mind, I will help you. The boy agreed, and King Mahmud himself cast the net, which, touched by the king’s hand, returned with a rich catch.

Heh, when you’ve been balancing between life and death for years, any fool eventually becomes a philosopher. So listen. A man went out to sow wheat in a field. The day was ending, and he hadn’t finished his work. He prayed: “Oh Lord, if only I had a helper, we could finish the sowing in no time…” Suddenly, a bear emerged from the bushes and said: “Come on, man, I’ll help you. It’s more fun together.” The man rejoiced and agreed. They sowed the field on time, and after embracing in farewell, parted ways. When harvest time came… the man couldn’t gather the crops in time. Again, the familiar bear appeared and said: “Come on, man, I’ll help you; together it’s easier.” They worked together and harvested a crop larger than the man had ever seen. The man said to the bear: “Let’s go to my house, Misha. You helped me sow and reap… let’s sit at the table, drink, eat, talk…” The bear agreed, pleased by the man’s generosity. They sat at the table, rejoicing, and the bear kept saying: “Man, if it weren’t for me, you wouldn’t have sown the whole field… if it weren’t for me, you wouldn’t have gathered such a harvest…” The man thought: “He’s big and strong, he might take everything and break my bones… no wonder he brags like that…” The man grabbed a pitchfork and stabbed the bear. The bear roared in pain and ran into the forest. Time passed… and it was sowing season again. The man went to the field, but sowed only half without a helper. At harvest, he gathered only half the crop. On his way home, the bear appeared from the bushes. The man reproached him: “Why didn’t you help me sow and reap, Misha? I gave you food and drink, and you?” The bear, pointing to his chest, answered: “Here it hurts…” The man examined the bear and said: “But it’s all healed, Misha!” The bear replied: “The wound healed… THE SOUL HURTS!!!”

Well — we’re not exactly super philosophers. Just lovers of reading things that really have value. So here’s one. Once, an unknown poet came to a king and said: — O King, unrivaled under the heavens! I have composed a hymn in your honor, and my creation outshines all the poems that other poets have ever made for you, like Sirius shines brighter than all stars in the night sky. With these words, he handed the king a sheet of paper with only the ruler’s name written on it. The king looked at the paper and got angry: — And where are the verses? — he shouted. — Have you lost your mind? You didn’t extol our royal glory, didn’t praise our victories and deeds. You didn’t even say that I created this whole kingdom with my own hands! Do you dare mock me? But the poet was not intimidated by the king’s wrath and replied with dignity: — All the royal wisdom, mercy, and power, and all the warrior glory are contained in this name, and anyone who reads it will immediately imagine all the deeds done by the king. For the name is the symbol of your kingdom, and it alone surpasses the thickest book of praise. So I was right in calling what my hand wrote an incomparable hymn glorifying you and your deeds!

Hmm, let me recall one of the last ones. Long ago, in the Middle Empire — that’s what China was called at the time — the best and mightiest fighters from all over the world gathered for a tournament. The contests were judged by a great Chinese sage. Finally, after thousands of fights, he chose the three greatest warriors and asked each of them how they trained. First, a huge, fierce-looking fighter stood up and said: — Every morning at sunrise I break trees, stones, planks — anything that comes in my way. Within a day’s radius of my dwelling, everything is turned to dust and desert. — Excellent, — said the sage. — And how do you train? — he asked the second fighter. A tall, lean man, more like a monk or ascetic, stood up. — Every morning at sunrise, — he said, — I devote myself to meditation. I bring my body under the control of mind and will, making it light and swift as thought. — Excellent, — said the sage. — And how do you train? — he asked the third warrior. The most ordinary, unremarkable-looking man stood up. If it weren’t for the fact that he recently won a thousand duels, no one would have thought he was a warrior, let alone a great one. — I don’t train at all, — said the third. — I just try to be present in everything I do.

Could it be that someone finally understood us? Well, there it is — enlightenment in life. So listen. There once lived a man named Godelo. He had a wife who constantly nagged him because his father interfered with their life, and Godelo couldn’t calm her. Godelo’s father, hearing his wife’s shouts day after day, called his son and said: — Son, I see I am disturbing your family’s happiness. Let me go live in the mountains... — Godelo, at first refusing, had no choice... The father began to get ready, then called his son again: — I have only one problem: I can’t make it to the mountains myself. Let’s have you carry me in a basket on your shoulders, and I’ll manage from there. — “Alright,” Godelo agreed. He placed his father in the basket and carried him to the mountains. After setting him down, he was about to leave, when his father called: — Wait, son, don’t forget to take your basket; your son might need it. Godelo felt ashamed, realized his mistake, repented, put his father back in the basket, and carried him home. ...And he drove the cruel wife out of the house.

Well, there’s a trend among experienced stalkers. We take turns getting hooked on one thing or another. Listen. A pilgrim walked thousands of steps to meet the Master and finally stood before him, asking to share the highest wisdom. — Then take a sip from this stream and describe the taste of the water, — said the Master. — I’ve already heard this and understand the Truth, — said the Seeker, a little disappointed. — Tell me, what do you understand? — asked the Master. — The taste of water cannot be described in words. Water must be drunk; one need not speak about it. The same goes for truth… — Now imagine — I am sitting by the stream, and a crowd like you stands in line for me to describe the taste of water, although many of them have already heard this description from other Masters.

Heh, after years of balancing between life and death, even a fool eventually becomes a philosopher. So listen. A girl was walking down the road, beautiful as a fairy. Suddenly she noticed a man following her. She turned and asked: — Tell me, why are you following me? The man replied: — O mistress of my heart, your charms are so irresistible that they compel me to follow you. They say I play the lute wonderfully, am versed in the secrets of poetry, and can stir the pangs of love in women’s hearts. But I want to confess my love to you, for you have captured my heart! The beauty silently watched him for a while, then said: — How could you fall in love with me? My younger sister is far more beautiful and attractive than I am. She walks behind me, look at her. The man stopped, then turned, but saw only an ugly old woman in a patched cloak. Then he quickened his steps to catch up with the girl. Lowering his eyes, he asked with a submissive tone: — Tell me, how could a lie slip from your tongue? She smiled and replied: — You, my friend, also did not tell me the truth when you swore your love. You know all the rules of love perfectly and pretend your heart burns with love for me. How could you turn to look at another woman?

Well — we’re not exactly super philosophers. Just lovers of reading things that really have value. So here’s one. A merchant sent his son to learn the Secret of Happiness from the wisest of all men. The young man walked for forty days across the desert and finally arrived at a beautiful castle on top of a mountain. There lived the sage he sought. But instead of meeting a wise man, he entered a hall bustling with activity: merchants coming and going, people talking in the corners, a small orchestra playing sweet melodies, and a table filled with the finest local delicacies. The sage spoke with many people, and the young man had to wait about two hours for his turn. The sage listened carefully to the young man’s explanation of his purpose but replied that he had no time to reveal the Secret of Happiness. He suggested that the boy stroll through the palace and return in two hours. — But I want to ask one favor, — added the sage, handing the boy a small spoon with two drops of oil. — Throughout your walk, keep this spoon in your hand so that the oil does not spill. The boy climbed up and down the palace stairs, eyes fixed on the spoon. Two hours later, he returned to the sage. — Well, — asked the sage, — did you see the Persian carpets in my dining room? Did you notice the park the head gardener took ten years to create? Did you see the beautiful scrolls in my library? Embarrassed, the boy had to admit he had seen nothing. His only concern had been to keep the drops of oil from spilling. — Now go again and explore the wonders of my Universe, — said the sage. — You cannot trust a person if you are not familiar with the house in which he lives. Calmed, the boy took the spoon and walked through the palace again, this time paying attention to all the artworks on the walls and ceilings. He saw the gardens surrounded by mountains, the delicate flowers, and the care with which each piece of art had been placed exactly where it needed to be. Returning to the sage, he described everything he had seen in detail. — And what about the two drops of oil I entrusted to you? — asked the sage. The boy looked at the spoon and saw that all the oil had spilled. — This is the only advice I can give you: the Secret of Happiness is to observe all the wonders of the world while never forgetting the two drops of oil in your spoon.

Hmm, let me recall one of the last ones. Once, in an Indian kingdom-state, there lived an old monarch who had spent his life pondering a purely Eastern question: what is the essence of strength? He finally decided to find the strongest man in his realm to learn from him. As a reward, the winner would receive a horse from the royal stables of his choice: white if he wished, black if he preferred. To solve this difficult problem of eternal choice, he gathered the wisest men of his kingdom and sent them on inspections across towns and villages. The monarch’s task proved extraordinarily difficult — the sages wore out countless pairs of sandals but could not find the strongest man. They arranged competitions and gave contestants many challenges, but none of the king’s subjects could claim the title of the strongest man in the kingdom. The sages had almost given up when, passing through a small village, they noticed a giant man lying on a huge bed in the middle of a large garden surrounding his house. “What enormous strength is hidden in this man!” they exclaimed and decided to test him with three trials. — Can you break a horseshoe with one hand? — asked the sages. He answered nothing and simply broke the horseshoe with his hand. — Can you uproot a tree? — they asked again. He took the ten-meter tree in both hands and pulled it from the ground. — Truly you are strong, man! But can you dam a river? — they asked a third time. The giant picked up a huge stone lying on the riverbank and threw it into the stream, stopping the flow. “Glory to the gods!” exclaimed the sages. “Truly you are the strongest man on earth! Choose any of the royal horses: black or white!” Only when the giant raised his hand to scratch his head, ready to announce his choice, did a small frail woman in dark clothing appear at his doorstep. “Husband, I warn you: if you choose the white horse, you’ll regret it! I cannot vouch for myself!”

Could it be that someone has actually started to understand us? Amazing. Enlightenment has finally come. So listen. Once, the wife of a prince approached Genghis Khan, asking him to free her relatives. Genghis Khan said: — Here stand your husband, your son, and your brother. I will release only one. Whom do you choose? Without hesitation, she replied: — My brother. — Explain to me why? The woman said: — I can find a husband, I can bear a son, but I cannot create a brother. After hearing this, Genghis Khan freed all three.

Well, after years of balancing between life and death, even a fool eventually becomes a philosopher. So listen. ...The gates of Zhongdu swung open, and into the richest city of the Celestial Empire, screaming wildly and brandishing weapons, the steppe warriors poured. Li Kui, known in Zhongdu as Snub-nosed Li, a vagabond without family or clan, who had appeared in the city out of nowhere just before the invasion — frail and disheveled Li Kui, limping, ran through the alleys of the western outskirts, hoping for luck. If great Guanyin would help him leave unharmed, he would become a shaven-headed Hashan monk in a saffron robe, living on alms and indifferent to worldly affairs — and let other fools give risky advice to the short-sighted Son of Heaven, the incompetent emperor, and let all those who wished, greedy ministers, filthy Mongols, and others, ravage the unfortunate Celestial Empire... Before realizing that Guanyin was looking the other way that day, Snub-nosed Li suddenly stopped, hissed in pain from his injured knee, and raised a short staff three elbows long to his chest. At the end of the alley, a tall Mongol in leather armor grinned, twirling a long spear with a crossbar under the spearhead. The fallen commander Li Kui, called Snub-nosed Li in Zhongdu and Blue Tiger Hou in the capital, took a deep breath and slowly approached the grinning steppe warrior, forcing himself not to limp. The Mongol waited until the frail fighter came close and lazily jabbed his spear forward. In a moment, the weapon nearly flew out of his hands, but when the astonished warrior managed to leap back, holding the spear with the point up, a hollowed-out staff with a hidden Blue Tiger Hou sword inside flew into his face. The city was already burning. Bitter smoke rolled through Zhongdu’s streets, preceding the looting warriors of the unstoppable Temujin Genghis Khan; smoke stung Li Kui’s throat painfully, and his leg ached unbearably, while the Mongol struck skillfully and fiercely, switching the spear tip and butt, moving constantly until Guanyin finally turned her benevolent gaze toward Zhongdu and the abandoned alley where Snub-nosed Li twice struck the Mongol’s wrist with his blade. Now all that remained was to wait. Wait and not get skewered by the spear, whose movements grew uncertain and shaky. And when the moment came — put all remaining strength into the final leap. ...He never released the spear, this unlucky fighter in light leather armor. Coughing and bent over, he sat against a wall, pressing the pierced side with his right hand, while his left still held the fallen spear. And above them, over the wounded Mongol and the powerless spear, stood Li Kui, former commander of the left wing Li, former Blue Tiger Hou, former Snub-nosed Li, future abbot of a monastery near Mount Wutai, a tired little man. He stood and watched the Mongol’s face grow paler and the cut wrists bleed less and less. The Mongol barely lifted his head — and their eyes met. Li Kui sighed, tore his cloak into strips, sat next to the Mongol, and began tightly bandaging the hands of the invincible Genghis warrior. Li did not know why he was doing this...

Heh, after years of balancing between life and death, even a fool eventually becomes a philosopher. So listen. The king’s daughter, with beauty like the moon, enchanted all who ever caught a glimpse of her. One day, a dervish, about to take a bite of bread, saw her and froze in astonishment. His fingers relaxed, and the bread fell to the ground. As she passed by, the princess smiled at the dervish. Ecstasy shook his body, the bread lay in the dust, and he nearly lost consciousness. He remained in this state for seven years. His home became the street, his neighbors the stray dogs. The mad dervish bored the princess, and her guards decided to kill him. Then she summoned him and said: — No union between us is possible. You must leave the city immediately, for my servants want to kill you. The unfortunate lover replied: — Since I saw you, life has no value to me. Innocent blood will be shed. But before I die, I beg you to fulfill my only wish, for you are the cause of my demise. Tell me, why did you then smile at me? — Fool! — said the princess. — When I saw how ridiculous you made yourself, I smiled out of pity — nothing more. And saying this, the princess disappeared.

Well — we’re not exactly super philosophers. Just lovers of reading things that really have value. So here’s one. Two wise men were conversing about love. One said: — The nature of love is to always bring suffering and misfortune, and the lover always suffers and agonizes. The other objected: — Be silent, you have clearly never tasted the sweetness of reconciliation after a quarrel, nor the joy of union after separation. No one in the world is purer and gentler than those whose hearts are pure in love, and none are fouler than those who are far from thoughts of love. In the beauty of a man’s heart — the reflection of the glow of love: If the heart is ugly, it does not honor beauty! Let the ignorant remember the saying of the wise: Where breed honors breed, — the kinship of souls is established.

Hm, let me recall one of the latest ones. Once lived a man, obsessed with mad love and exhausted by the pangs of desire. The beauty of the one he loved was unmatched, even compared to the beauty of heavenly maidens. People said of her that her beauty outshone both the Sun and the Moon. Moreover, her incomparable beauty was dangerous, not only because it could inflict deadly torments of love, but also because this beauty was both intelligent and merciless. One day, the man, thinking himself alone, spoke to himself: — My God! Her face is fairer than a rose, her form more slender than a cypress! Her whole appearance is brighter than a peacock, and her walk more graceful than a pheasant’s stride! But it turned out that his beloved was nearby and had heard his words. She appeared before him and said: — You probably meant to flatter me with your comparisons, but they only anger me, and I find them shameful. You compared my face to a rose, but where in roses have you seen such beautiful eyes and eyebrows as mine? You compared my form to a cypress, but does a cypress possess my mobility and spirit? You compared my whole appearance to a peacock, but has anyone ever burned with love observing a peacock? You compared my walk to that of a pheasant, but who in the world delights in recalling a walking pheasant? — Forgive me! — cried the lover. — Out of my foolishness, I imagined that you could be described with words! These words enraged the moon-faced woman even more, and in anger she said: — For this foolishness of yours, I will torment you! Then the lover fell at her feet, saying: — O incomparable otherworldly Beauty! All my awkward words stem from my true and pure love for you. There is no malice, no reproach, not even a complaint, and if they seemed inappropriate to you, be kind and do not judge me harshly for this fault; consider it a sign of my ignorance and inexperience, and I shall perish from shame and disgrace! And he sought and uttered words of repentance so long that the beauty calmed down and abandoned her intent to take revenge on the man who loved her.

Could it be that someone has actually started to understand us? Amazing. Enlightenment has finally come. So listen. In ancient times, there was a thief. One day he robbed the treasury of the padishah. The padishah immediately sent all his investigators after the thief. The thief was caught and the loot taken from him. Then the padishah asked his chief vizier: — What should be done with this thief? — Gather all the townspeople and, before everyone, execute the thief, — replied the vizier. The padishah had a daughter. He asked her: — Is what the vizier said correct? — The vizier judged correctly, — replied the girl. Then the padishah asked the middle vizier: — What should be done with the thief? — Gather plenty of wood, place the thief on it, and set it on fire. Again the padishah asked his daughter: — What do you think of the vizier’s words? — Your vizier judged correctly, — she replied again. Finally, the padishah asked the youngest vizier: — What should be done with the thief? — Dress the thief from head to toe, give him everything so that his stomach is always full, and release him. The padishah asked his daughter again: — What do you think of the vizier’s advice? — Your vizier judged correctly, — the girl replied. Then the padishah said: — You said that each of the three viziers judged correctly. What do you mean, explain to us. And the girl replied: — The chief vizier, who said the thief must be killed, was probably born a butcher. Your middle vizier, evidently, was a brick-burner. And the youngest vizier is noble by his very nature. The padishah checked, and the girl was right. The thief was treated according to the advice of the youngest vizier: he was well-fed, given money for expenses, and spared death. The youngest vizier was made chief vizier.

Hm, let me put it this way: among experienced wanderers, there’s a tendency to get drawn to one thing or another. Listen. Once, a gang of robbers went on a raid. After looting many items, they began to divide them among themselves. All shares were equal, except for a dress made of the finest threads—which was considered the worst share—and it was given to the weakest member of the gang. That weak man, upon receiving his share, became furious. He started shouting that he had been cheated. When he went into the city to sell the dress he received, all the wealthy and noble merchants offered him large sums of money. What he alone had received turned out to be twice as valuable as the combined shares of all his comrades. He then rejoiced and leaped with happiness. This is similar to ordinary people who give a small alms (part of their income) without knowing what reward it may bring. When they are reborn in heaven and experience immense bliss, they start to regret—they regret not giving more generously (their entire wealth). They are like the man who, receiving a great sum for the dress, rejoiced. With almsgiving, the same principle applies: great reward comes from small deeds. That is why, alongside their joy, there arises regret for the incompleteness of what was done.

Heh, after years of balancing between life and death, any fool eventually becomes a philosopher. So listen. Once, there was a family: husband, wife, and daughter. The family was extremely poor, barely making ends meet. The husband and wife began to think about how to become wealthy. — We live in poverty. Let’s find a rich man, settle near him, watch what he does, and imitate him in everything. So they did. They demolished their hut and built a new house next to the rich seth?y’s home. The seth?y made his workers cultivate the fields and garden, then sent them to sell the harvest. The poor couple tried to imitate the seth?y wherever they could, working in the fields and selling part of their harvest at the market. Gradually, their affairs improved, and their wealth grew. The husband and wife decided to thank the seth?y, from whom they had secretly learned the art of managing a household. The husband took a handful of gold and went to the wealthy man. — Why have you brought me gold? — asked the seth?y, surprised. — I was poor. My wife and I decided to demolish our hut, settle near your house, and imitate you in everything. Our affairs quickly prospered, the family grew wealthy, and now we offer you this gift in gratitude. — Since you yourself believe you gained all your wealth thanks to me, then it is my wealth. You must give me all your property, and I will decide what share to allot to you. — But I was not ungrateful and brought you gold. Had I known you would demand all my wealth, I would have brought nothing. The seth?y and the former poor man argued at length. Finally, they turned to a judge. The judge could not resolve their dispute and led both to the king. The former poor man bowed and said: — Your Majesty, I settled near the seth?y’s house, observed how he managed his affairs, how he sent his workers to cultivate and trade. I imitated the seth?y wherever I could and achieved prosperity and success. In gratitude, I brought gold to the seth?y, who helped me unknowingly, but now the seth?y demands all my property. I ask you, Your Majesty, to resolve this matter justly. The king addressed the seth?y: — Did this man recount the case correctly? — Entirely correct, Your Majesty. Understanding that the seth?y was overly greedy, the king said: — Seth?y, do you have children? — A son, Your Majesty. — And you? — asked the king, turning to the former poor man. — A daughter, Your Majesty. — To prevent conflict between you, I decree that both families should be joined by marriage. Then there will be nothing to divide. The seth?y and the former poor man bowed low to the king and departed, ready to follow the wise royal decision.

Well — we’re not exactly super philosophers. We’re just lovers of reading what truly has value. So here’s one. There lived a hermit in the desert. Once, in his homeland, he had been a skilled carpenter. But life, by chance, cast him into the desert. A wealthy man learned about this master and sought him out, saying: — I’ve heard you are a skilled craftsman. I want to order an oak table and chairs for my hall. I’ll come to pick up the order in a month and will pay well. And he left. When the appointed time came, the wealthy man returned to the master and asked: — Where are my table and chairs? — Yes, I am a skilled master — but that alone is not enough. To fulfill your order, I also need wood and tools, which I do not have... Desire alone is not enough: one also needs the means...

Hm, let me recall one of the latest. In Shamil’s army, a letter was sent to a soldier informing him that his wife had died. Shamil himself received the letter. He decided to test the soldier and sent for him. When he arrived, Shamil said: — You received a letter saying your father has died. — In that case, the roof of our house has collapsed, and I need to go home, — replied the soldier. — No, it was not your father, but your brother who died. — Then a wall of the house has fallen; I must attend the funeral. — It turns out it was not the brother, but your mother who died. — Then the house is collapsing. I must go home. — No, not your mother, but your sister has passed away. — A flower has fallen from my heart — I must go home. — I was testing you. Actually, your wife has died. — If so, the whole house has collapsed, and nothing remains. Now there’s no need for me to go home.

Is it possible that someone has actually started to understand us? Incredible. Enlightenment has come, it seems. So listen. Two people were traveling towards each other. One was hurrying to help his benefactor, and the other was rushing to a celebration. They met on a narrow bridge and refused to give way to each other. They argued and finally turned to a judge. The judge could not resolve the matter and sent them both to the king. The king asked: — Explain to me why you did not want to yield to each other? One of the disputants said: — Your Majesty, I was hurrying to a celebration and met this man on the bridge, but he did not let me pass. The other said: — At one time, a man did me a great favor. Now he is in trouble, and I was hurrying to help him. That is why I did not yield. The king delivered the following decision: — The one who was hurrying to the celebration must compensate the one who was rushing to help his benefactor.
What interesting things can you tell me about life?
Quite fascinating.

The regiment I served in had a fairly large auxiliary farm: a sizable greenhouse, a pigsty, and even a horse named Bouquet. In the pigsty, a conscript soldier, a Gypsy by nationality, looked after the pigs. One winter, a sow farrowed. Everyone rejoiced, counted the piglets, and accounted for them, but at night one died… In the morning, the deputy commander came to check on the sow, and the Gypsy (I no longer remember his name) presented him with the newly deceased piglet. What could be done? They wrote one off. The Gypsy, not being a fool, didn’t throw the dead piglet away but hid it in a snowdrift. A day or two later, he took one live piglet for himself (over the years I forget whether he ate it with friends or sold it locally), and presented the deputy commander with the piglet he had hidden. Another piglet was written off. He tried the same trick again, but it failed. The regiment commander was very angry when he was informed about this. He personally went to the pigsty. For some reason, he especially disliked the radio receiver in the room where the Gypsy slept — a big, old wooden-cased radio. He made the soldier carry it from the pigsty to the parade ground, where the whole regiment was assembled, and then march around the parade ground with the radio. When the deputy commander asked, “How long?” he replied, “Until Nikolai Slichenko sings…”

A large diesel submarine was at sea on a combat mission. According to the daily plan, so-called anti-emergency drills were conducted — actions meant to prevent actual emergencies later. In the central post, the deputy brigade commander was peacefully gnawing on a cracker. He was known for his rather fiery temperament, but that day he was leaving everyone alone. Among the submarine crew was a warrant officer named Pozhar, the kind of person everyone always needs. For some reason, that day he was needed by the deputy brigade commander. When it became clear that Warrant Officer Pozhar was not at his station, a command went out: — Report the compartment in which Warrant Officer Pozhar is! The command was given and quickly forgotten in the rush. Suddenly, from the diesel compartment, a report came over the loudspeaker: — Central post, Pozhar is in the fifth compartment! (But you can’t see capitalization in oral speech!) The central post reacted instantly. Short bells rang, followed by words that made even the thick-skinned flinch: — Emergency! Fire in the fifth compartment! It was a scare that made your soul tremble. There is plenty to burn in a diesel compartment… The boatswain shifted the horizontal rudders for surfacing, the acoustics operators listened intently to the horizon (hoping not to collide with anyone on the surface), and the hold crew prepared to deploy a very serious fire suppression system in the fifth compartment. The central post shouted: — Fifth! Fifth, report the situation! What is burning there?! The diesel crew replied calmly and embarrassedly: — Nothing is burning. We were simply ordered to report the compartment where Warrant Officer Pozhar was, and that’s what we did. After the emergency alarm was over, a debriefing took place using all naval terminology. Then the deputy brigade commander announced over the loudspeaker: — Attention, submariners! From now until the end of this deployment, this fellow’s last name will be Ivanov!

In the early 2000s, I actively participated in organizing and preparing various charitable events, where the main advantage was the opportunity to meet serious people on an equal footing in a non-commercial setting. And, of course, to help children. At one such event, held at a departmental hotel, I helped a woman of the “I can do everything but want nothing for myself” type. In exchange for a massive amount of preparatory and fundraising work, I was promised several extremely valuable introductions to sponsors. In fact, at every large charitable event, there are four main groups of people — actual sponsors, event organizers along with various performers and “support staff,” children or other beneficiaries, and the category I want to focus on — the freeloaders. Not all freeloaders are complete freeloaders — some contribute a small amount to the children’s fund. But understand, if a banquet for sponsors costs $100 per person, you are not counting on 200 rubles from a lottery ticket. At the start of the event, I scanned the hall and noticed each of the four groups. There were not many freeloaders this time — control was in place — but they were present, as the tables were piled with food and drinks, and the program included star performances and other delights. The evening was split into two parts — the first featured stories of the beneficiaries, children’s performances, and a charity auction. The auction reveals the true freeloaders, as they never buy anything and try not to be seen by the active auctioneer, often slipping away quietly. One freeloader caught my attention immediately — a simple-looking man of about 30 with a small ponytail, completely rustic in appearance, looking like a child from a small Siberian town seeing the Kremlin for the first time. He didn’t even buy a lottery ticket, which completely astonished me. Since there was so much organizational work — all on my young shoulders (I was 22) — and it was important work (“these are children!” as the movie line goes), I gave it my all. During intermission, the woman I was helping kept her promise and introduced me to several very serious people from Russia and beyond. When I asked if anyone was really influential, she hesitated and said she couldn’t answer. The contacts I received were already enough for significant achievements, so I decided to let it be. During the second part, performers appeared — the ones I had helped organize. Somehow, everything aligned, and the event was a huge success — the sponsors were excited, I was sweating and hadn’t eaten all morning. The freeloader I had noticed earlier enjoyed himself like a child, clapping and eating heartily. After the event, guests were asked to donate to the collection. Most gave more than expected, but the freeloaders at best put in a small amount, and my rustic guest — nothing at all. The organizer was happy. As the guests left, she told me: — “Alright, you helped me a lot. I’ll show you ‘a hero of our time.’ But you must promise not to tell anyone about him or bother him — he is extremely shy and never shows off his help. This man financed the construction of new buildings for our orphanage for 300 children and the entire surrounding infrastructure, and even connected a gas pipeline from the nearest settlement. I gave my word. We left the hotel together, and she pointed to the rustic young man getting into a used Volga. Witnessing this construction firsthand was revolutionary for me. At that moment, I truly understood what it means to be a ‘serious person.’ Since then, whenever I see freeloaders at events, I remember those innocent, loving, simple eyes and try to spot such people. Sometimes — I find them. And immediately, I understand everything.

This happened a long time ago, at the very beginning of the 1990s. I was working as a young specialist at a well-known institute that developed OUR supercomputers. They didn’t pay much, so we had to moonlight. One time, we went to the city of Perm for some extra work. There, a cooperative had sold a batch of Chinese junk (players, toasters, a couple of TVs) to a local factory called NefteOrgSintez as employee rewards. Naturally, the defect rate was over 50%. So, the story goes: After stocking up on these technical wonders, we were sitting at the airport in bad weather, snow mixed with rain. The flight to Moscow was delayed for the second time. We were drinking in the restaurant. After a while, I realized I couldn’t drink anymore — I might die! I thought I’d walk in the cold to sober up a bit… and I ended up on the airfield. I saw some military transport being loaded. — Where are we flying? I asked. — To Moscow. — Take this with you. — Carry three bottles and get on board. — Gut! I dashed after the vodka, somehow with a bag containing a couple of pairs of underwear, and back. The flight was very fun. I think the crew was drinking with us too. (I vaguely remember a pilot shouting: “Takeoff is nothing! The main thing is to land!”) We landed in Zhukovsky. They transported us outside the airport in a UAZ covered with a tarp. Then I don’t remember. Around four a.m., I was sleeping in a soft bed at home. The phone rang. My wife reluctantly picked up. Through my sleep, I heard her trying to explain something for about five minutes… Finally, she shoved the phone to me with: “Tell him this!” — Hello, I said. — Who is this? Dimon, is that you? — Yes. Pause. — Holy crap! Bastard! Scoundrel! Etc. — We turned the entire Perm police upside down! A man with money disappeared! Traffic cops are checking cars leaving the city. The plane left without us (it was a regular flight)! And you, fool, are sleeping on a soft couch at home! Anyway, the next day I took a sick leave. Just in case…

This was long ago, when planes flew drunk and there were no terrorists. We had a guy on watch, let’s call him Vasya. He’d spend one month at work drinking, one month at home fasting, because he couldn’t really party continuously. On the last day of his watch, the guys celebrated, and in autopilot mode, along with other unlucky fellows, he boarded a plane. At the bus collection point at the base, his backpack was lying there, and some good-natured pranksters, as always, slipped a valve into it. The valve is a thing 25 cm long, 8.3 cm in diameter, weighing about four kilos. All loaded, flight, reunion with his beloved wife, salary immediately confiscated, junk from the backpack went for laundry, and on the floor lay the metal piece. — Vasya, what’s this for? Who wants to admit that they got tricked and carried an unnecessary piece of metal for three thousand kilometers? — It’s a necessary item for the dacha, you go to the store. His wife went to the store. Vasya, hungover, looked at the device, and disgustedly threw it out the window. Steps in the hallway, and the item is placed on the table — by its knock, it reminds one of a medicinal concoction. Vasya opens his eyes. — Vasya, you carried a spare part so far, and I just came from the store, and look — there’s the same one under the window. On the table proudly stands Vasya’s valve…

I served my country as a radio operator-radio mechanic at a backup command post “Zhilny” from 1978-1980. I had a habit of saluting officers without wearing my hat. We had a political officer major — I don’t remember his name, but he was a real jerk. Our commander, “Filipok,” Lieutenant Colonel Filippov, a commander from God, called me in. I entered the office and, as required, reported, raising my right hand to my temple. Since I was on combat duty, I hadn’t put on my garrison cap after removing my headset. With Filipok was the political officer. He said: — You don’t salute an empty head. I should have stayed silent, but the devil made me reply: — Sometimes a head in a peaked cap with an emblem sounds like an empty jug. Filipok smiled, and the political officer grimaced. I thought my lip was guaranteed to be in trouble. After hearing the order and replying: — Yes, permission to execute, — I left the office. I don’t know how other officers found out about this incident, but three hours later they started predicting I’d become a general. By the way, all officers, when meeting us conscripts after saluting, shook hands, but one political officer never offered his hand.

An ordinary hose is just a long rubber tube. “Pretending to be a hose” for a Soviet soldier was like playing dumb, passively sabotaging orders from commanding officers. Sometimes this happened at the expense of fellow soldiers, so the “hardcore” — those who took service seriously and did everything to earn positive points in the eyes of the command — didn’t like hoses very much. Yet there were hoses who were legitimate, like the Russian spy Stierlitz: “Why bother catching him? He’ll get out of it anyway.” These were treated with affection.
Few conscripts didn’t want to get home as soon as possible. Most quietly dreamed about it. Some tried to fake illness. In our unit, there was a legend about a young soldier who, upon entering the barracks for the first time, immediately sought out the heaviest weight, and upon finding it, began exercising his right arm. He did this every free minute and was encouraged for his zeal by the company commander. Just a month later, the young man went to the medical officer and declared: — Sir, my left arm is shrinking… The medical officer personally measured both biceps but recorded only the difference, as instructed. — Let’s check the trend, soldier, — said the major, slightly surprised. After a couple more weeks of intense workouts, the difference increased, and by the end of the month, the discharged soldier went home to Grodno… For some reason, he had no followers; even very dumb soldiers understood that an epidemic of shrinking arms would not go unnoticed.

This story happened when my uncle served in the Soviet army, around the 1980s. Two soldiers once brought sand to their military unit in a ZIL dump truck. A dump truck is very convenient: drive up to the spot, pull the lever, and the loose material falls in a neat pile according to gravity. But since it was late autumn with rain and frost, the sand in the bed had frozen slightly. It didn’t want to pour out properly. However, the driver and his partner, being models of indifference, reasoned that at the highest point, the sand would fall anyway like a punishment from heaven. Usually, that’s how it goes, but they didn’t account for the truck being on a slight incline. The unmoving sand significantly raised the truck’s center of gravity, causing the ZIL to tip over, finally releasing the sand. In the cabin, naturally, the soldiers fell on each other.
The story is fairly ordinary — dump trucks tip over often. The most interesting part happens immediately after the fall: a crowd of soldiers, who were watching the unloading with interest, rushes to the truck cabin, smashing almost all the windows with boots and tools and pulling our two comrades out, grabbing anything they could. One ended up with a torn uniform, the other scratched on the face. It turned out the observing soldiers were scared of an explosion and decided heroically to rescue the drivers.

I don’t remember who told me this story, but it went like this. It took place in the Soviet army. In one region of our vast Motherland, there was a military unit; I don’t remember its specialization — infantry or artillery — but that doesn’t matter. Imagine a quiet, dark summer night, the whole unit sleeping a deep, strong sleep. In one of the barracks, as required, the duty sergeant (a senior conscript with just over a month left to discharge) and a young sentinel (fresh recruit) were on watch. The young man stood on a stool, fighting sleep with all his strength, while the sergeant dozed in the so-called “Lenin room.”
Notably, the sentries and duty soldiers were equipped only with bayonets, strictly accounted for like any other weapon. At one point, the young soldier realizes that either his stomach isn’t used to army food or he caught an infection — or both. If he doesn’t act immediately, all the contents of his intestines will end up in his boots. Whispering in agony, so as not to wake the unit, he calls the duty soldier. The duty soldier, muttering and cursing all the “freshmen” who won’t let him finish his term peacefully, asks the reason for being disturbed. The young man quickly, clearly, and concisely explains his predicament as military regulations require. The sergeant, grumbling and cursing young men and their delicate stomachs, lets the duty soldier go to the toilet and takes the post himself.
Civilization’s benefits like plumbing hadn’t reached this unit; there was a separate, very remote wooden toilet with three holes, no partitions. The young soldier runs to the toilet at breakneck speed, unfastening his uniform on the way, and positions himself over a hole. At the climax, the bayonet, issued for the defense of the Motherland, slips from his belt and falls mockingly into the hole. Realizing what happened, his urges intensify threefold. Scenes of being punished for losing his weapon flash through his mind, and worse — what the sergeant would do.
Summoning all his courage, he trudges back to the barracks and tells the sergeant everything. Knowing he would be punished at least as much, maybe more, and his discharge delayed, the sergeant feels the urges in his own stomach. But a Russian soldier, as they say, is invincible and finds a way out. After a short thought, the sergeant finds what he considers the only solution: he dresses the young man in a full protective suit (for chemical weapon protection), a gas mask, and sends him back. “Go, dive, find a solution, and don’t come back without your bayonet.” The young soldier promptly carries out the order.
But bad luck strikes: at that very moment, the duty officer, a Soviet army major, also needs to use the toilet. He goes to the same facility and positions himself calmly. Now imagine the major’s reaction when something in a gas mask, covered in excrement, with a bayonet in hand, emerges from the neighboring hole. According to the duty officer’s assistant, the major stormed into the duty room with wild eyes, one hand holding his unfastened pants, the other waving his service pistol, reeking of excrement and shouting something about saboteurs or evil spirits. The whole unit was raised on alert. After investigations, both soldiers received 15 days in the guardhouse, and the major was sent to calm his nerves.

Here’s how it happened. In one military unit, training jumps were being conducted. The jump itself, with a standard airborne parachute (D-5), wasn’t particularly difficult. You roll out of the aircraft, count in your head: “501, 502, 503” — and after falling at least 60 meters, try to spot your deployed parachute canopy above your head. Once located, you land carefully. Everything usually went according to plan — jump, canopy opens, land safely.
But one unlucky soldier had a problem. Yes, the canopy opened, but he didn’t reach the ground. A gust of wind carried him into the pines surrounding the drop zone. Picture it: hanging like a puppet in the branches of a massive pine, about 10 meters up. Reaching the trunk is impossible; climbing the lines is possible, but then comes the same problem — how to get down. Usually, you deploy the reserve parachute (on the chest) and use its lines to descend. Being young, several advisors immediately rushed to help. Enjoying the scene, everyone shouted in unison: “Pull the reserve!”
What followed looked like a movie. The already frightened paratrooper grabbed a line cutter, cut the main canopy lines in one stroke, and, while falling, pulled the reserve parachute ripcord… P.S. I must note — the record for lowest-altitude parachute jumps was not set that day.

In my military department group, there was a student nicknamed “Yellow.” He had a unique talent for swearing, apparently from birth. And he ran into a typical student misfortune — during self-study, an officer caught him enthusiastically playing cards with another slacker…
As punishment, they were made to stay after class, given a bucket of quick-dry paint, brushes, and ordered to paint all the desks and chairs that had been scribbled on by students. The work was completed, handed over to the duty officer, and brushes cleaned…
The next morning, the cleaning lady came to mop the floors. The first thing she did was flip all the chairs onto the desks. Then she went to the teachers’ room, returned with the deputy head of the department, and showed him the familiar three-letter inscriptions on the unpainted sides of the chairs. That same day, the two mischievous painters appeared before the department head.
— What on earth are you doing, students? — he mildly rebuked. Yellow suddenly stood at attention, clicked his heels, and reported: — Sorry, Colonel, it slipped out! — How many times did it slip out? — the colonel was astonished. — As many times as it slipped, I wrote it, — Yellow finished calmly.

Back then, I worked at an instrumentation institute in Moscow on Kutuzovsky Avenue, near the monument to the pilot Valentina Grizodubova — anyone passing by surely noticed the granite woman in a flight helmet. In the Soviet era, we developed airborne radar systems; after the USSR collapsed, we mostly switched to commercial orders — someone had to eat.
Then we got an assignment from the Ministry of Defense to create a “black box,” the purpose of which I won’t reveal. During testing, we had to spend several hours in the technical compartment of an A-50 — not exactly sipping brandy in a Boeing seat, but that’s another story. The acceptance act required a test for airtightness. The enclosure had a tricky shape, but for simplicity, imagine a cube with a 1-meter edge. Soviet testing methods: the cube is lowered with a winch into a giant thick acrylic tank, about three meters deep. If bubbles appear, the item fails, including the electronics inside.
The tank looked like an abandoned factory. The water was rusty and murky. The winch had been stolen long ago, so a worker had to submerge the cube manually. It’s a large, sealed aluminum cube. One man alone couldn’t do it. We engineers refused to help — our job was schematics, not playing with cubes. The soldiers, however, took over. Three of them rolled up their sleeves, climbed onto the ramp around the tank, and shouted, “One, two, three!” pushing the stubborn cube.
They almost did it. At the last moment, the cube flipped onto its side; everyone except the cautious worker fell into the tank. Those nearby jumped back to avoid the dirty water, splashing everywhere. Chaos ensued: soldiers struggling, stepping on each other, a floating meter-long metal cube swinging and hitting anyone it could. Curses and cries filled the air. Half a minute, maybe more, until the fallen worker came to and helped the others.
The soaked soldiers were taken away, presumably for 40-degree calming treatment, and the “presentation” ended. We smoked, discussed the events, and went back to work. Surprisingly, no one got in trouble. Later, the black box was accepted into service, even though some tests never got completed. P.S. Before the officials arrived, the device underwent all tests except the infamous tank dunking; that prototype may still fly on a Russian A-50 today.

Once I ended up in a hospital with a banal case of sciatica. I was around 25 then, a bit of a dandy, a ladies’ favorite, quite the Don Juan. To diagnose me properly, they decided to X-ray my spine. For this seemingly simple procedure, a pre-X-ray enema was required — to prevent intestinal contents from obstructing the view of any herniated discs.
Armed with a large roll of toilet paper, I entered the designated room. A large room, with an elderly lady in a white coat. “Lie down here on your side,” she said. I complied. After administering the procedure, she left. I noticed a toilet in the corner — no partitions, just a solitary white fixture, inviting. Positioned on a pedestal, climbing it made one feel like a proud eagle on a cliff. And inside, the process was underway.
I dashed to the door, searched for a lock — none. Peering into the corridor, I realized I wouldn’t reach the main men’s restroom. So, like a white-tailed eagle, I perched on the toilet.
It turned out the hospital was full of female medical interns that day. The sight: a young eagle perched proudly on the porcelain, gripping the paper roll, while the spine preparation continued unabated. Every minute, a head popped in: — Is Mary-Ivanovna here? Oh! Another: — Mary-Ivanovna… oh!
How many interns saw the proud bird, I don’t recall. Many, each prettier than the last. It didn’t exactly thrill me.

This happened in the Army. I studied at the Irkutsk Military Aviation School with my brother. We’re twins. Not identical, but hard to tell at a glance. Once, during exams, my brother ended up in the infirmary.
How could he take the exam if he wasn’t released? I refused to take it for both of us, but lying in for him while he took it — why not? So I took the exam, rushed to the infirmary, swapped clothes with my brother. I’d never been in the infirmary and had no idea where anything was. My brother explained: go to the second floor, find room X, find bed Y in row Z — it’s yours. I found the bed, lay down, ready to rest.
After about 5 minutes, a nurse entered: “G-ov, time for your shot!” I bolted, yelling: “What shot? I’m healthy!” She said: “I don’t know, I’m waiting in the Procedure Room.” I realized I was stuck — they were going to punish me for my brother. I thought to hide in the restroom.
About 20 minutes later, I decided to leave. Crawled out, planning to sneak back to the bed. A lady in a coat saw me: “Oh, G-ov, carry these containers to the cafeteria.” I had no clue where it was, grabbed the containers, wandering hopefully. She hovered behind, pressuring me. Following the signs, I finally found the cafeteria. I dropped the containers and dashed back to the bed. Just as I settled, the nurse returned: “G-ov, how long do we wait? Come on.”
And that was it. I received my dose of penicillin for my brother. We laughed about it for a long time, especially me.

This funny story took place in the glorious Soviet Army. Cadets were ordered to clean a howitzer. Everyone was bustling with the brush, running back and forth, the brush sprayed generously with a special cleaning solution containing SEVEN alkalis!
Since this was happening in our country, the cadets cleaned the weapon without protective suits or even gloves. Suddenly, one of them needed to “take a pee.” He went, returned to formation, and started feeling increasingly uncomfortable! Looking down, he noticed that his member was swollen, red, and about four times its normal size!
With no one around remotely familiar with medicine, the cadet was simply helped to wash the affected organ, wrapped clumsily in bandages, and sent to the infirmary.
No doctor was present. Only a young nurse sat in the reception. Casting a stern glance at the wounded soldier, she asked: — What happened, serviceman? — Well… see for yourself…
Embarrassed, the cadet showed his unzipped fly, from which the bandaged organ protruded. — My God! What did you do to it!? — the nurse exclaimed. — Cleaning the gun…
He was cut off mid-sentence by the nurse’s wild laughter and the timely arrival of the doctor — the entire infirmary was paralyzed!

I served in Germany. In Russia, one could sneak away on leave or absent oneself, but there, even if allowed, where would you go? Germans everywhere! One day we went on a field exercise. When we arrived, we heard via the grapevine that not far away was a lake with a nudist beach, something unimaginable in the mid-1980s — the mere thought of it was like five kilotons of Viagra.
— Awesome! — I said to Igor, and began planning.
The next evening, with the officers temporarily absent, we decided to seize the chance. Of those brave enough to go, only three remained — Igor, Musa, and me. The rest chickened out. As real scouts, we planned to reach the beach unnoticed, disguised as Germans. The lake was small; we swam around to the other side and intended to emerge naturally.
On the opposite shore, we stripped off our cotton uniforms. Doubts crept in about remaining unnoticed.
Musa, covered in body hair from head to toe, with tattoos and all sorts of soldier folklore, looked more like a Yeti than a German. But being sharp-sighted and hot-tempered, Musa, already glimpsing some female anatomy, refused to remove his blue cotton briefs initially. After some effort, we persuaded him.
Musa was impressive. Midway across the lake, we swam like real Germans; the first naked figures and female voices appeared. Suppressing natural instincts, Igor and I reached the shore, lying on the sand as if nothing happened. Musa, however, remained waist-deep in water, mouth wide, eyes wide.
Nearby Germans smiled. From Musa’s chest hair, a parachute with Cyrillic letters peeked, but Musa still believed he looked German. We signaled him to come out. He shook his head and replied in German: — Nein! After fifteen minutes standing frozen, he drew the attention of half the beach, causing laughter and encouraging German phrases, blushing under his body hair.
We had to rescue him. Approaching from behind, we carefully pushed him onto the shore. Musa resisted, swearing in Russian, but finally fell onto his stomach. Already exposed, we dragged him onto the sand amid laughter and applause. He remained on his stomach, attracting local women’s attention. Igor and I later visited the nearby village a few more times, without Musa.
P.S. Musa ran back into the water very quickly.

We had a pilot in our regiment famous for making forced landings in a jet with a dead engine four times. The fourth was the most entertaining.
Here’s how it went. The airfield was on the Lena River. He took off normally, but at 600 meters the engine died.
Jet planes without engine thrust fly like bricks thrown out a window. What to do? Below, the city; straight ahead, the Lena River, ice thick, winter — he put the plane into a dive straight for the river. He was heading to a range, bombs and rockets under the wings — quite a sight.
He belly-landed, sliding on the ice — fast! About two kilometers, then opened the canopy and saw a man fishing, wrapped in a coat, oblivious. The pilot yells: — Hey, buddy, you’re in my hole!
The fisherman turned, saw the jet, and ran so fast that even a jet afterburner wouldn’t catch him. Such was the story in our glorious Air Force, when fuel was plentiful...

This actually happened to me in the Caucasus Mountains in the spring of ’95. I was studying journalism at the MESU (Kuban branch) in Krasnodar and often went hiking with a backpack, accompanied by other amateur (and overconfident) tourists. Gear: at best a few meters of rope, sometimes an ice axe. We were climbing Mount Achishkho, near the future Olympic sites. Twenty years ago, it was quite wild.
The climb began on the second day after a night at a semi-derelict tourist base. The mountain itself wasn’t difficult; good for tourists, not mountaineers. The main challenge was the weather: late April, early May, snow thick on slopes and plateau, many deep cracks, avalanche risk, plus strong wet snow wind. I frostbit my left hand; my right hand, holding the ice axe, was fine. That’s the prelude.
At one point, about 100 meters from the pass, the slope at 60°+ — I decided to climb left of the main group. Off I went, realized my mistake five meters later. Returning horizontally was dangerous; snow underfoot was wet, slippery. I slid downhill like a bobsled, no fear, just surreal detachment. Tried braking with the ice axe, then speed increased, bounced, hit my head — cap flew off, glasses — gone. My “eagle” eyesight of -7 meant near blindness. Tumbling, I slid about 200 meters.
Finally, lying face down on a small plateau, I had to force myself to stand. Trembling, hands and legs numb, but not in pain. I understood the trip was over; without sight, only one way down — with a guide. Ten minutes later, Commander Gennady Ognev emerged from the mist.
— Alive? — I think so… — Now…
He went down a few meters, returned with my cap. — Here, don’t catch a cold.
I asked, slurred, if he’d seen my glasses. Gennady laughed: I had dug snow like a tractor, dodging cracks like a slalom skier. Archaeologists would find my glasses in 25,000 years. Then the incredible: he bent down, pulled my GLASSES from under my left boot — intact!
— Boy, you’ll live a hundred years.
I’d never have believed such luck if it hadn’t happened to me. Thirty minutes later, on the pass, Svetlana fed us chocolate by hand, our own hands useless. That night, in an abandoned weather station near Achishkho, I saw a real ghost.

Once we were celebrating a girl’s birthday. I, along with a few responsible lads, arrived on time, but the rest of the guests were late. For obvious reasons, nobody waited for the latecomers — the party was in full swing.
Those who arrived were simply fined and welcomed into the group.
By the time about 30% of the guests had gathered, everyone was quite drunk. The birthday girl went to the bathroom. A couple of minutes later, we heard screams from inside: — Open the door!!!
As true gentlemen, we could not leave the lady in distress and, amid cheerful hooting, carefully removed the bathroom door from its hinges. The first words we heard from the girl sitting on the toilet in the proud eagle pose were: — IDIOTS! OPEN THE FRONT DOOR…

This happened during the second campaign in the Chechen Republic. A group of heroes successfully completed a mission and reached Mozdok without losses (those who know understand) and were waiting for a flight at the airfield camp.
At that time, the Mozdok airfield area had a transfer base, a large tent village (or a small tent town, depending on perspective) with streets, alleys, a headquarters (town hall), a canteen, etc. The heroes were assigned two tents on the edge of the village, but everyone piled into one (collective spirit). They bought some anti-stress medicine at the local pharmacy and went into nirvana (the flight was scheduled for the next morning).
Among the heroes was a dog handler specializing in mine detection, with a female spaniel named Katya. At dawn, he discovered the dog was missing. Thinking she had gone on a dog business and needing to relieve himself, he stepped outside, calling softly at first, then louder — Katya, Katya, where are you? — and searched the nearby streets. No dog.
Then a dreadful thought hit him (strengthened by lingering anti-stress medicine): the dog was lost! With the flight in a few hours, he could not cancel it — total disaster! Calling out in desperation — Katya, my girl! — he reached the main street near headquarters and the canteen.
Suddenly, a girl’s head poked out of one tent, playfully saying — I’m here! At the same time, Katya appeared from a neighboring tent, guilty for the mischief (probably following the smell of food). A male tear welled up in the handler’s eye, relief, but the soul demanded revenge: — You little… bitch, sneaking into other tents!
Within a second, the camp erupted in wild laughter. It turned out that coincidentally, the local serviceman was celebrating the birthday of a local soldier, Catherine, which lasted until morning. Nobody expected that a namesake would temporarily appear in camp and create this memorable mishap.
I’ve heard that some stalkers served in hotspots. Do you know anything about the Afghan and Chechen wars? Maybe some eyewitness stories?
Thank you.

A friend recently told me a story. I’ll tell you too. The intelligence department of the 5th Directorate of HAD managed to insert an agent among the enemy. In Pakistan, he was recruited and sent back to Kabul with orders to blow up a school, carrying two kilograms of plastic explosive, about a meter of fuse, and two detonators. He brought it all to HAD and reported the assignment.
KGB advisors asked me to examine it. I broke off a piece of the plastic explosive, lit a match — people jumped aside. The plastic ignited with bright flames. The fuse also burned properly. Quality excellent. To test the detonators, one had to be used, but advisors didn’t want extra noise. They pondered the next step. Blowing up the school was forbidden, but the agent had to be tested. The school, near the airport, was a two-story wooden structure, like a barn.
I suggested: — Guys, give him to me. Imported explosives are for foreign operations. I’ll make a bomb from Soviet plastic explosive. The blast will be strong, windows everywhere, school unharmed.
The senior advisor squinted cunningly: — Maybe you want it as a trophy? — Pff! — I left, offended.
Next day, I first learned from Afghan colleagues about the night enemy sabotage. A patrol noticed someone tampering near the school cable manhole. The stranger fled. Soldiers leaned over the manhole; then the explosion occurred. Two soldiers injured. Power cable shorted, wooden school burned down.
In the morning, I congratulated the advisors on the successful operation. They turned pale and ran to their subordinates. Reported to their chief. The chief philosophically remarked: — At least we successfully disguised our agent!

I heard something. Our mentor at the Cordon told us by the campfire. Let me recall. Unarmed Khoshal approaches the gate of a villa where our hero is hiding. A jeep arrives. Khoshal is almost point-blank shot in the neck. Seeing him fall, a soldier fires an RPG-7 at the jeep. Two militants creep along the wall. Wounded and concussed by a nearby grenade, Khoshal grabs the pistol he was just shot at with and shoots the militants. Khoshal is carried away. The vehicle burns. They hide in the villa.
Rashid’s friend appears, orders a grenadier to fire at the windows. Both grenades overshoot the roof, exploding somewhere in town. Rashid sends the driver with keys to his office. Under his couch are four disposable M-72 American grenade launchers (my gift). The driver returns. Rashid carefully fires each launcher at a window. Screams, smoke. The panicked prince in the basement calls the MGB, asks to stop the fire. Many dead. He surrenders.
At Khoshal’s hospital bedside, two guards stand constantly — the prince had ordered his men to eliminate the operational regiment command along with advisors. He overreacted. We were innocent. But leadership sounded the alarm: — Forget a few advisors; worse if we lose Kandahar!
The prince cannot be killed, must be calmed. He is flown to Sochi with two loyal wives, taking two bags — one of dollars, one of foreign trade cheques. The king publicly rebuked his foolish son. Returning from the Union, the prince went back to Kandahar. Relatives of killed militants pursued him. He fled across the border, later sending a tearful letter to a KGB advisor inviting him to… Pakistan.
Khoshal recovered quickly. Remarkably, the 9mm Makarov round passed between his trachea and carotid artery without hitting vital nerves, vessels, or bones. Rashid ended up in the awkward role of the blood enemy of his former friend and drinking buddy. A showdown is inevitable.

The older guys used to tell stories by the campfire. Recently, one shared this one. A narrow path, with a small irrigation channel alongside. Thick bushes all around. Nothing visible beyond a few meters. The path twists and disappears around a corner. An ambush head-on!
A 50-meter length of detonating cord, with a hand grenade tied every 5 meters — ten grenades in total. All this is placed in the water channel and camouflaged with grass. This time, we’ll detonate using an electric detonator. The grenade rings are pulled, their triggers held with string tied through the cord.
Once the enemy lead guard approaches, the detonator presses the button. Explosion! How would a normal fighter react if it goes off half a meter from his feet? Naturally, he falls on the path. Even if the mujahideen quickly recover and look around, it’s too late — grenades lie all around.
Crackling of grenades! Now our machine gunner must pin the remaining “spirits” to the ground with long bursts through the bushes while the detonator runs away.
Since the lead guard in the “green zone” moves within visible distance (5–10 meters) from the column’s core, our grenade-mines are bound to hit 5–17 “spirits.” Trophies are unnecessary. Our goal is to strike hard so they won’t walk our street again. For training, we use RGD-5 grenades. In combat, we’d use F-1. F-1’s full blast radius is 3.7 meters; to be safe, we reduce it to 2.5 meters. So if a grenade goes off every 5 meters in an enemy column, even if prone — it’s no joke.
Tests on the range showed detonating cord can throw F-1 grenades 0.5 to 15 meters.

Many didn’t like to recall this. From a seasoned soldier, I’ll share a tale. As the workload at the Paghman school increased, so did accidents. None fatal. A few times it happened to me too. The main cause was that we used only captured ammunition, often damaged in the enemy’s wet pits (no other supply).
For example, every third or fourth 82mm mortar round didn’t fire. Imagine the nerve-wracking extraction! Chinese RPG-2 rounds caused trouble too — their black powder charges tended to get damp, so twice instead of firing, the powder burned slowly and grenades fell meters away. One, thankfully, didn’t explode. Another, exploding, injured three, including me. This was during my second mission.
While showering at the villa, I noticed many small pimples scratching my palms. On closer inspection — tiny shrapnel. Had to pick them out with a needle. My bunkmate Alexander Ivanovich saw my green-stained stomach and said: — Go to the doctor, could cause sepsis! — Yeah, the doctor will report to the chief, I’ll get scolded and forbidden to work.
A large fragment was stuck in a leather wallet in my pocket — it got stuck in a thick stack of Afghanis. Had it hit slightly to the right — disaster. Next day, advisor Vitaliy told a fresh joke: — Bek returns from a mission. His wife asks what gift he brought from Afghanistan. Sad Bek pulls a dried male organ on a string from his pocket.
Another source of accidents: I was preparing “consumables” at the special forces school. The front required many trained fighters. They shouldn’t fear nearby explosions or whistling bullets. For comparison, two instructors from Moscow: in six months one prepared ten Afghans for a special lab. I trained over a hundred. Not that theirs was worse. Afghans observed: explosions scare either the totally ignorant or the highly professional. My students were in between — semi-professionals, fearless.
Anything could happen in training. Once, someone shot under his feet with an AGS-17. Another time, a heavy 82mm mortar fell 20 meters away. A cadet accidentally loaded a hunting cartridge instead of a standard propellant.
A nightmare: an OZM-72 bounding mine went off in an Afghan’s hands! If detonated, 2000 balls would mince us. Luckily, no detonator — it flew 20 meters, glanced his forehead, knocked him out, and everyone froze for a few seconds.
Cadets often got scratches throwing RGD-5 and RKG-3m grenades. Two explosions occurred when collecting unexploded ordnance. Always wear protective goggles.
One incident: teaching grenades to a special lab group. Many captured grenades. Divided by fuse type: instant and delayed. Instant: RKG-3 anti-tank, cumulative with parachute, RGO, RGN, and a defensive Egyptian grenade — cylindrical, enamelled, metal strip in middle, pierced by pin with lead seal. Pull pin, throw, spring unrolls, explodes on impact, shredding metal gears into nail-sized fragments.
Delayed: F-1, RGD-5, RG-42, with UZRGM fuses, also English, American, Belgian, German, M-46.
I explained grenade mechanisms to Afghan comrades and took them to the range. Instant grenades: throw high and far, hide immediately. Second class: can be handled less carefully. One fool mixed up — threw an Egyptian instant grenade nearby. Fell into mud, didn’t explode; spring didn’t fully unroll. I told the duty officer to destroy it; sergeant didn’t. Later, an Afghan lieutenant picked it up, spring snapped, he threw it aside. Explosion! Severed one leg to the knee.
With M-46 in the 40th Army, a paratrooper lieutenant thought it was a smoke grenade. Pulled the pin, threw it in a smoking room. Result: a dozen dead. Officer shot himself.
I averaged 1–2 accidents per month. My psychological state? Probably fatalism. What else could one do? Cadets must never see fear. Instructor must be fearless. Fatigue accumulates, not relieved by alcohol. Nervous breakdowns sometimes without reason.

Better not have reminded me — a sore topic. But I’ll tell one story. The operational regiment commander (our battalion had grown to a regiment) invited advisors to the “Kurban Bayram” celebration. Leadership, fearing incidents, forbade participation. Afghans were upset. The regimental logistics officer lamented: — We bought snacks for 20,000 Afghanis, and you didn’t come! A grave insult.
Sergey, as a Muslim, tasked me to resolve it. At that time, I was on a provincial mission, so supposedly unrelated. I swore to partners the advisors were innocent, and we felt the incident too. Ready to make amends.
Next day, the whole group visited the regiment. In the van — besides liters of cognac, vodka, champagne, and a box of dry wine — we carried several firearms. At the regimental range, we spread tents and laid out a German MG-42 (“Hitler’s saw”), PK, M-16, plus our RPK and AKS-74.
Two translator-soldiers loaded belts. After three obligatory toasts, I announced a contest: a shot glass of alcohol for hitting the target! Afghans fired bursts. Gradually, everyone got competitive. Out of the corner of my eye, I noticed Din Mamad slowly backing away at the start of shooting. While everyone competed, he managed to knock over an extra glass, crawl back, and return to the firing line.
We all liked the MG-42: 1300 rounds/minute, almost no dispersion — a burst hits a small target at 800 meters!
After shooting and sipping champagne, hugging partners, we returned to headquarters. Incident resolved. Din Mamad — big, slightly stooped, from the commando unit, huge hands like rakes — whispered: let’s go to Kabul to see girls. He had a decent “chicken coop” there. But he only had the strength to reach his bunk. Collapsed, eyes open. I closed them: — Sleep peacefully, friend Din Mamad…

A friend recently told me a story. As soon as we entered the sector controlled by Chechen fighters, she called over their commander and spoke at length, pointing at me. Then she said to me: “They considered you an FSB agent here, wanted to either shoot you or take you to the mountains, and I protected you.” Of course, I was full of gratitude, but thinking logically — who could have called me a security officer? Maybe they just didn’t believe her or didn’t have time to deal with me? Her son, Ruslan, after ’96, gladly helped me move around Ingushetia — first in his “Zhiguli,” then in a sporty Porsche. Talking to him wasn’t much about, except women, but very convenient for work. Still, he was an acquaintance. A “brother”...
The day before October 4, when he offered to give me a ride to the checkpoint near Mozdok, one thing struck me. Ruslan mentioned having a beautiful girlfriend he visited almost every night, and then calmly added that he was married with two children. I asked: “Does your wife know? What does she say?” He calmly replied: “What can I tell her? She cries.” There was such cruelty in that calmness that it unsettled me.

I heard something. I’ll recount it from an eyewitness. On the morning of October 4, I overslept. Barely freshened up, I grabbed my things and went down to the hotel lobby. Ruslan was already there. I felt awkward that he had to wait; I fussed over checking out and paying for all phone calls. Neither while driving around Nazran, with Ruslan stopping at various addresses, nor on the Nazran–Malgobek road, did I remember the passport I left at the hotel front desk. Nor did I suspect anything. I would have reached Mozdok by noon and maybe even accredited at the federal forces press center preparing to enter Chechnya. With luck, I could have been in a forward unit by evening. With luck...
Most likely, the idea to “sell” me arose in Ruslan’s mind only after returning to Nazran, when he started stopping at various addresses again. I can imagine a dialogue: “— There’s a journalist in my car… — Really? Who? — Seems from ‘Moscow News.’ — Alone? — Yes… — Wait… All right… Go to Magomed’s home, tell him everything, let him gather his men quickly. Got it? Then go to the hospital, find Said. He’ll settle with you. Decide where to meet…”
Ruslan earned his money well — I hadn’t thought of any of this. The day was summer-warm and sunny, I filed one report, it went in the paper, and five more days of assignment were ahead — a chance to distinguish myself and do something notable for the newspaper. Mood was excellent...

The older guys used to tell stories by the campfire. The following is from an eyewitness. The first days in captivity, I recalled the moment of capture with horror and pain. Stupid, bastard scum, greedy creatures thinking themselves supermen! Federal forces were already bombarding with full force, fully ready, and the situation didn’t allow for kidnapping — it wasn’t ’97 when bandits, feeling untouchable, were snatching journalists one by one! Federal troops, whatever their faults, were not the rabble called “presidential guard” or “state sharia security units” by Maskhadov! No matter the state of the Russian army, imagining officers themselves participating in abductions like the Chechen bandits of sovereign Ichkeria is impossible!
Recalling that dreadful moment when the Judas left me alone in his car near the republican hospital while supposedly visiting his girlfriend, and a plain white “six” with Ingush plates stopped nearby, I realize this swift operation succeeded only because I froze for a split second. If I had shouted or started a fight, it would have gone differently. Not far away, around the corner, was a busy area — a bus stop, some shops. This relaxed me completely. When a blond man in military uniform, without insignia, peeked through the lowered car window: “Your documents! Passport check,” I thought nothing bad. I just got out and walked to the white “six”...

Many didn’t want to recall this, but it happened. It’s our life. My former partner told me this story. Passing me into the back seat of his “six,” the bandits hissed in my ear: “Shut up, bitch! I’ll kill you!!!” and jabbed me in the side with pistols to convince me. Air in the plastic bag ran out fast, and I began suffocating, especially as I kept trying to shout. From this moment, time crumpled like paper.
I remember the car racing through Nazran streets, someone’s strong hands tore off my belt bag with all documents, money, and wristwatches — a gift from my mother, in which I had dived in Crete in ’98. Someone pressed my head into their knees. Outside the bag, the bandits spoke in terse phrases in Chechen or Ingush. In shock, I yelled nonsense: “Basayev will kill you for me! Let me go or regret it! Don’t shoot! Then kill me, don’t take me anywhere!” The bandit jabbing me yelled: “One more word — I swear, I’ll shoot you like a dog right here!” and hit the gun butt above my ear. Not hard, but very painful.
