Soon I weakened from lack of air, begged for a breath. The car sped on asphalt. I rasped: “You’re Chechens! Better cut me than suffocate!” One bandit replied: “We’re not Chechens. We’re Arabs,” and all four laughed in disgusting, deliberate voices. They lifted the edge of the bag; the air inside, smelling of five sweaty men, felt refreshing like sea breeze.
Then transferred to another car: “Get out fast, bitch, I’ll kill you! Here! Here!” — then to a third. Bandits switched cars. In the second, they were “kind” — lifted the bag so I could breathe, allowed smoking. Third car: “bad” again. I remember the soft ground under a fruit tree, lying face down, hands and shoulders stiff from ropes; two automatic riflemen forbade movement to ease the pain. After five minutes or an hour, a car approached, bandits kicked me up (with tied hands, standing was incredibly hard) and drove me like a sheep, shouting angrily. Dark when they brought me to the dungeon. My new home (for just two weeks, I later learned) was an unfinished garage among ruins and rubble. Hours before, I was free, planning steps and asserting rights. Now, I couldn’t believe I was a hostage — no longer quite human — and any bandit could do anything to me with impunity.

Better not have reminded me. A sore topic for many. Here’s what our commander told us. Now, after the wave of panic, pain, and constant tension subsided, I often think: could it have been different? All the circumstances leading to that fateful moment on October 4, 1999, around 3–4 p.m., when four bandits forcibly took me in their car, now seem a chaotic mix of coincidences and absurdities.
If the day before I hadn’t suddenly decided to visit acquaintances in Nazran… If the “Assa” hotel clerks hadn’t been such kind, friendly girls, whom I didn’t want to offend (two days before capture, I jokingly but strictly asked: “Will my passport be safe with you?” Girls offered to return it; I said: “No, it’s safer with you — I always lose everything”)… If not for my cursed forgetfulness… If Ruslan, the Judas, had an old Zhiguli instead of a sporty two-seater Porsche, reaching Mozdok quickly and comfortably…
These “ifs” make up any business trip — hot zone or safe country. But no matter how many assignments I had at Moscow News, circumstances always aligned perfectly. Perhaps luck had run out, and during that last trip to Ingushetia before a new big war, something bad was bound to happen. Lucky it was “just” a kidnapping. Lucky there remained a tiny hope, even in the darkest days.

A friend recently told me a story. Or rather, it’s a firsthand account. Thinking now of people who knew me since ’96, called me “nephew” and “brother,” and cold-bloodedly led bandits to me, I feel, besides dull anger, revenge, and hatred, a quiet, calm disappointment. Not so much in their integrity, but in their intelligence. How foolish and greedy must one be to deliver me into bandits’ hands without disguise or covering tracks!
In the dungeon, I found only two explanations for such treachery — either they thought me a complete fool unable to make basic conclusions, or they were told I wouldn’t survive. The second explanation made me ill. I won’t name the “guides” — mother and son, though their surname appeared in Moscow News. This pair is now in a detention center; my kidnapping is just a minor episode of their family’s wild activity. Mother was already suspicious in ’96 — too businesslike, “mechanical,” with extensive connections in international humanitarian circles and among Chechen fighters. She claimed to have a selfless, noble nature, devoted to freeing Russian POWs, repeatedly facing death. To be fair, she wasn’t too afraid when we arrived March 7, ’96, in war-torn Grozny. At the time, I didn’t understand why she did it.

I heard something. More precisely, I read the last accounts of a former commander. Listen. April 4, around 3 p.m., the BMP gunner pushed us: “Get up, we’ll move soon!” Indeed, after 15 minutes, we moved… 600 meters to the road took an hour and a half. The “spirits” were above, shooting at us like a shooting range. From our company, 2nd and 3rd platoons went to the road, 1st and company HQ remained 200 meters back; 2nd and 6th companies flanked Goiskoye from the left.
Our men decided to advance across the road, requested cover; my SVD didn’t fire — a grenade fragment in the bolt. I disassembled the rifle; found other metal fragments in the receiver. Checked all, tested rifle — worked.
Our group moved to the road, throwing grenades at the spirits. Initially, a machine gunner-assault rifle team secured the spot. Under cover, the rest advanced. We drove the spirits from the position — about twenty. Retreating, they left five for cover — grenades shredded them. Dug foxholes didn’t help.
We secured our position. Meanwhile, 6th and 2nd companies fought fiercely in the “green zone.” Eleven, including platoon leader filling company commander duties, fell there. Two weeks later, spirits requested bodies removed as decomposition progressed. Meanwhile, we blocked the right flank of 2nd and 6th companies. In one burrow, found a live “Czech,” who killed the boy who found him. Tried flushing him out — no luck. Poured kerosene, set fire, threw grenades — nothing. Apparently, the burrow path was angular, so he survived.
While the fuss continued, main company forces arrived. They, having lost four men, failed to cross the field. Our platoon leader argued with company commander over late arrival. Commander shouted: “No clear orders from battalion commander!” Platoon leader: “Battalion commander was wasted. Had to decide myself!” While they argued, we inspected Chechen trenches, finished the wounded. Then ordered: “Fall back!” Absurd, of course. One draftee tried explaining on radio to hold position; no one convinced.
Securing 6th company’s exit, we retreated. Self-propelled guns of 324 MSP shelled Goiskoye; we split into two groups. First withdrew with all gear, second covered. Withdrew normally, but incidents occurred. Last tank, number 420, covered everyone. During assault, spirits burned two BMPs — one ours, one 6th company’s. Tank hit burned BMPs for good measure. Then a gunner from “two” fired at it! Tankers said: “Not a big deal, but when your own hits you during retreat…” Shooter unknown.

The older guys used to tell stories by the campfire. One of them, a former soldier, shared this. Messy and boring — new recruits from some airborne brigade buzzing around. Three days with paratroopers, we turned Khankala into a fortress. Now a bit calmer. Spirits stopped silly night raids, we weren’t chased without reason. Timid generals allocated a whole Internal Troops brigade and an airborne battalion to guard tiny Khankala; a motor rifle regiment promised. Where would they be stationed? All rhetoric — nothing to do now.
I go to the radio operators, need to know events, or war ends and I’ll be unaware. — Hi, “Broom.” — “Moscow,” got a smoke? — Anything new? — Marines, paratroopers, motor rifles, Sofrinets are active. For a moment, Dudaev, the asshole, doesn’t give up — life sucks, sun’s a damn lamp! — Heard news about our brigade? — Nothing specific, standing by, guarding “jackal” command…
I try another wave on the radio; static breaks into “Chukchi” guttural speech, not Russian, whatever — we continue. I catch a motor rifle unit reporting they cornered “spirits” in a five-story building. No callsigns, pure swearing — some ensign Zinoviev had sexual relations with a BMP in an unimaginable spot…
Good thing about booby traps — another spirit raid on our “Behemas” noticed in time. Fat “Broom,” during my radio interception, fell off chair while trying to put on body armor and grenade pouch, crashed to floor with loud noise. Would be funny, if not sad.
In seconds, I rush to my vehicle, spot platoon leader behind rebar, enthusiastically firing at spirits foolish enough to appear in open. Either we’re truly capable soldiers, not a herd of deer as the commander claimed, or spirits were stupid, but we held Khankala well — almost no casualties. Two KIA in six days in January ’95 in Chechnya — not bad, though unfortunate they happened.
After a few minutes, we assembled a combined platoon, swept the area — no spirits found nearby, placed a few traps and “lights,” nothing should roam without our knowledge. Another day in another war… Who needs all this…

Many didn’t want to recall this. This is from an eyewitness. Having crossed the militants’ defensive lines in Grozny, we entered neighborhoods fully controlled by the “spirits.” Army camouflage and our unshaven, darkened faces with sunken cheeks gave us a chance to pass for militants in case of a sudden encounter with the “warriors of Ichkeria.” The militants’ mess was similar to the chaos in our own troops’ positions. Pass systems and passwords often didn’t work amid the general confusion, and shouting “Allahu Akbar” was sometimes enough to be accepted as one of them.
Our goal was reconnaissance behind the militants’ battle lines and disrupting enemy communications by planting mines to create chaos and panic in their ranks. We moved only in the evening, but before dark. Nighttime movement through the city was dangerous, as both sides doubled their vigilance. At night, we hid in foul-smelling basements or abandoned houses in the private sector, selected after prior reconnaissance to have good views of all approaches to our hideout.
Dusk was falling. We had been circling this district for two days, unable to find a passage to the neighboring area teeming with armed militants wearing green armbands. We had to wait out the night and move at dawn to the adjacent street. Earlier today, I noticed that the exit marked on our map had been turned by militants into a fortified position against Russian tanks, blocked with large debris — wrecked cars and a half-burned bus.
A sixth sense told me the improvised barricade was surely guarded by “spirits,” so rushing in blindly was unwise. After setting guards and a lookout, we rested on a pile of broken bricks. The bricks dug into our backs, and inner tension prevented sleep. Turning onto my stomach, I lay on bent arms — uncomfortable, but forcing myself to sleep, as tomorrow would be hard.
It wasn’t sleep. Closing my eyes, I fell into a trance of forgetfulness, somehow continuing to monitor the surroundings. I awoke suddenly. Silence. But my stomach felt cold and uneasy. Moments later, I simultaneously heard the crunch of debris, heavy footsteps, and a cautiously prowling flashlight beam.
“Spotted! Where’s the lookout? Already with a slit throat?” — I was close to panic. I couldn’t hear my comrades’ breathing. They weren’t asleep, crouched like me in the brick pile. The flashlight carriers — surely a militant patrol — stopped near our hideout.
A silence that felt like a lifetime. Heart in throat. “Calm down. This morning I read the Lord’s Prayer; God will protect me,” — this thought didn’t exactly calm me, but it helped overcome paralyzing, animal fear and prepare for action.
Automatic rifle in hand. Fingers of the other hand found the cold, ridged surface of an F-1 grenade, meant for one purpose — not to be captured alive.
Footsteps began to recede. “They didn’t find us.” Tension eased. Yet concern for the lookout comrades remained.
A signal. “Our people!” Two shadowy figures slid to us. Militants had appeared suddenly from a basement we hadn’t noticed at first. At dawn, we cautiously left our hideout, leaving a few “presents” — two MON-50 mines. Exiting the basement and surveying the area, we found a barely noticeable passage into the basement, which turned out to be a throughway to the street we previously couldn’t access.
After mining this passage, which militants might still attempt to use, our group slipped behind the “spirits” near the tram depot. Staying long was unwise; the depot and surrounding area teemed with militants as Makhadov’s headquarters was there, and they could stumble on our “presents” and realize intruders were in their territory. Collecting reconnaissance data visually, setting and camouflaging mines, we prepared to withdraw. The retreat route planned and approved by command was poor. Or rather, while behind enemy lines, the situation had changed. Contact lines had shifted; in some areas militants created new strongpoints. We had to recon further and find new retreat routes.

Better not have reminded me. A sore topic for many. Listen to an account from a veteran. Although our group included men who had lived in Grozny before all these events, navigating the ruined city was extremely difficult. In some areas, positions from five days ago might now be held by our troops, creating a high risk of coming under fire from federal forces. While there are no “smart” deaths, it’s still foolish to be shot by your own.
To prevent this, constant reconnaissance of the terrain was necessary. I witnessed the consequences in January 1995, when our reconnaissance groups accidentally encountered our own forces. A similar group under Vadim ran into “Volgograd troops” (8th Army Corps under General Rokhlin) in Grozny. Lack of coordination proved tragic: Vadim’s group was mistaken for the enemy, and fire was opened. One member was killed. Vadim jumped from cover, shouting, “Don’t shoot! We’re friendly!” When detained by approaching troops, it turned out he had no documents. Vadim, attempting to explain, was brutally beaten and nearly executed. After investigation and requests for confirmation to higher command, he was taken in critical condition to the “Northern” hospital.
I stood by his bed, pained listening to his broken, stammering words, repeating: “Y-y-you understand, I got c-c-concussion in A-A-Afghanistan, and h-h-here it’s the end…” Two hours later, he was transported by special helicopter to Mozdok, then to a hospital on the “mainland.”
Running through a small park, through an arch, we entered a courtyard. It seemed from another life. The war had spared it so much that ruins on neighboring streets made the arch look like a gate between worlds. The silence was unusual.
Yet the deceptiveness of silence in war became apparent within minutes. Trusting it, we made a mistake that nearly led to a tragic outcome. We violated fundamental rules of movement in enemy territory: “Trust neither eyes nor ears. Trust only instincts. Check seven times…” and other principles, never to relax.
From a second-floor window of a neighboring house, someone called in Chechen. We froze. Not receiving an answer, fire was opened. Either poor shooters or our “animal instincts” kicked in — only one in our group received a light glancing wound in the side, as we quickly took cover behind high concrete slabs.
To avoid wasting ammunition, we refrained from heavy firing at the enemy in the building. Under cover of the same slabs, replying with short bursts and single shots, we bought time to make the “only correct decision.”
Through basement windows, we entered a three-story house behind us, one by one. Swearing at our carelessness, we first helped the wounded. Two went on reconnaissance; the rest took defensive positions. Recon returnees reported that Russian troops storming the city were nearby. Apparently, the proximity of federal forces prevented the militants who fired at us in the courtyard from taking further action against our group in the house. Or maybe it was an enemy group similar to ours, not meant for prolonged open combat. In any case, we quickly established communication with the command of federal units, and within an hour were sent on BTRs to the “Northern” sector.

A friend recently told me a story. Now I’ll tell you too. The “ceasefire” turned into a bloody tragedy the very next morning. At 10 a.m. on Staropromyslovskoye Highway, militants detonated a remote-controlled mine planted on the roadside. Shrapnel from the exploded 152-mm howitzer shell (tank and howitzer shells were used by militants as improvised mines) pierced the armor of a BTR, instantly killing the driver. The uncontrollable BTR, moving at 70 km/h, jumped to the opposite side of the highway, knocked over a concrete lamp post, and stopped, covered in blood and brains of the 101st Internal Troops brigade soldiers on board.
Four soldiers died instantly, the fifth, whose legs were blown off, died on the way to the hospital, the sixth died on the operating table. Nearby, an elderly Russian woman passed, covering her face with her cloak, unable to hide her tears. She had to hide them from the crowd of Chechen youth gathered at the intersection, cheerfully discussing the event.
The same day, the Main Department of Internal Troops (GUOSH) received five militants captured at a checkpoint. Dressed in black clothing resembling uniforms, they carried weapons, half of which were equipped for silent firing. Leaving Shali after an operation by federal troops under General Shamanov in May 1996, this group attempted to infiltrate Grozny.
The leader of the group, as revealed during investigation, was also the commander of a reconnaissance unit from Shamil Basayev’s detachment. The captured militants testified that they came to Grozny to carry out terrorist attacks. Evidence included a list of Chechen government officials with identifying features and office locations. Interrogations revealed they were also ordered to evacuate the families of militants fighting in the mountains by June 10. All this suggested that the “ceasefire” was being used by the militants for their own purposes, hardly peaceful. Tensions in Chechnya increased daily.
The refusal of Russia’s military and political leadership to live by the rules of an undeclared, yet real war led to the tragic events in Grozny on August 6, 1996.

I’d heard a bit. This story comes to mind. It’s old. My company was called up to disarm a Chechen stronghold, while the “Vovans” (MVD Internal Troops special forces) did the main work and we only covered them. When the Vovan commander was brought the village elders, he demanded they hand over 24 AKs within two hours. One elder got cocky, acting like Lukashenko. He claimed the village had a legitimate self-defense unit but lacked rifles, so the federals had to immediately provide 20 more AKs. The Vovans were slightly taken aback, but we didn’t hesitate. The brazen elder received a burst to the bread cutter (machine gun) in front of everyone, and while his legs twitched, the others heard the delicate request: not 24, but 100 rifles, and not in two hours, but one. The Chechens complied in forty minutes, handing over exactly 100 rifles.
The moral: politics and diplomacy work with partners within certain limits. With a partner who’s lost all restraint, a different set of tools is needed.
I saw bus columns reeking so badly you couldn’t approach within 100 meters, packed with bodies of slaughtered Russians. Women cut evenly by chainsaws, children impaled on signposts, intestines artistically draped over fences. We Russians were wiped off our land like dirt under nails. And this was 1992 — two and a half years before the First Chechen War.
Then it got even worse. Militants came to a stanitsa, cleaning out Russians. At night, cries of people being raped and killed in their own homes could be heard. No one came to help. Everyone was on their own. Some even tried to justify it ideologically: “my house, my fortress.” I heard that then — the man who said it is dead; his guts were draped over the fence of his own house. That’s how we, the cowardly and foolish, were killed individually. Tens of thousands of Russians were murdered, thousands enslaved or taken into Chechen harems, hundreds of thousands fled Chechnya in just their underwear.
This is how the Vainakhs solved the “Russian question” in their republic. And they succeeded only because we were worthless, complete trash. We’re still trash now, though less liquid — steel grains are starting to appear among the filth. When these grains unite, Kondopogi happen. Few so far, but the Vainakhs are clever — real forest janitors. Their cultural mission gradually restores Russian people.

Yes, older guys used to tell stories around the fire. There were a couple of veterans. Listen. In the war, fate brought me together with a Jew — Lev Yakovlevich Rokhlin. Initially, we weren’t supposed to participate in the New Year assault. But when contact with the 131st Motorized Brigade and 81st Regiment was lost, we were sent to help. We broke into the 8th Army Corps’ positions under General Rokhlin and reached his headquarters. That was the first time I saw him personally. At first glance, he didn’t impress: hunched, cold, cracked glasses… Not a general, but a tired agronomist.
He tasked us with gathering the scattered remnants of the Maikop brigade and the 81st regiment and bringing them to Rokhlin’s reconnaissance battalion. That’s what we did — collected scared troops from basements and brought them to Rokhlin’s scouts. About two companies in total. Initially, Rokhlin didn’t want to use them, but when all other groups retreated, the 8th Corps remained surrounded in the city center, against all militants. Then Rokhlin lined up these men opposite his troops and gave a speech. I’ll never forget it. The gentlest words he used were: “fucking monkeys” and “faggots.” At the end he said: “The militants outnumber us fifteen to one. No help is coming. If we are to die here, let each of us be found under a pile of enemy corpses. Let’s show how Russian soldiers and generals die! Don’t let me down, sons…”
Lev Yakovlevich is long gone — taken care of without you. One less Jew, right?
I told you about Rokhlin’s speech. What happened next I didn’t tell. Then came a terrifying fight — from my 19-man platoon, only six survived. When the Chechens broke into our positions and it came to grenades, realizing we were doomed, I saw real Russians. No fear, only a mix of furious calm and detachment. One thought: “Father asked us not to fail.” The wounded bandaged themselves, injected themselves with promidol, and kept fighting.
Then we engaged the Vainakhs in hand-to-hand. They ran. That was the turning point in the battle for Grozny. It was a clash of characters — Caucasian versus Russian — and ours proved stronger. That’s when I realized we could do it. This inner strength exists; it just needs to be cleansed of the accumulated filth. In close combat we took prisoners. They didn’t whine — they screamed in terror. Then we intercepted a radio order from Dudayev: “Do not take 8th Corps scouts or VDV special forces alive or torture them; kill and bury them as warriors.” We were very proud of this order.

Many didn’t want to recall this. It was a tense time. Between Goyskoye and Komsomolskoye were orchards, and behind them the militants had ATGM positions. Their operator was excellent, hitting targets three versts away. He even tried to target the 324th regiment’s checkpoint, firing from four kilometers out. One day the commander assigned us to take out that ATGM. We assumed the position was well-guarded, so eliminating the operator had to be silent. We asked the commander to issue a VSS “Vintorez” sniper rifle. He did, but someone had to pick it up with the head of the missile-artillery service. We went to the regiment on a passing convoy.
The situation in Goyskoye: the militants couldn’t break our resistance because we outmatched them in firepower, and we couldn’t take Goyskoye due to small numbers. Anyway, we pressured the militants, who warned that if any unit of the 245th Regiment approached Goyskoye, they would block the gorge.
In Nizhniye Atagi, waiting for the convoy, it arrived at 1:30. The convoy included returning draftees and those leaving for family leave. (They weren’t accounted for, so casualty numbers were hard to determine later. One Ural with about 20 draftees burned after one “Shmel” hit — supplies inside, boys sitting on top, all burned…)
I checked the convoy mail — none. Going back, I saw four “Nalivniks” in a row; one had my friend Arkasha. He was deputy platoon commander. Lucky! I asked, “Space in the cabin?” He said, “Come see.” I moved a vodka package and fit inside.
Around 14:00 we moved. At 14:10, passing Chishki and entering the gorge, shots fired. Arkasha noted only women and children ahead. Previous intel: if men, women, children — safe; if only women — ambush.
The convoy stretched along the serpentine. Trucks barely maneuvered. We joked along the way. Passed Yarishmardy, the lead was around the bend. Then explosions ahead — tank turret thrown, second blast elsewhere, third between us and lead truck. Hood torn off, windows shattered. I was concussed first time. Arkasha already out, I tangled in the doors, eventually fell out. Intense fire, but I managed 15 meters away. Found a ditch, lay low. A conscript soldier beside me. First shock passed, assessed situation — not good. Trucks stopped. Platoon fired all directions, enemy positions unclear. Arkasha fired at a militant under his truck.
A grenade landed in a truck behind us, burning it. Estimated the blast would hit us — high danger. Saw someone 170 meters away, aiming new grenade — I shot him. Others firing from trench, one I shot but unsure if killed. Militant crawled uphill on all fours, first shot scared him, second flipped him over.
Arkasha removed burning truck. Smoke obstructed aim; we decided to retreat. Covered sectors, I fired forward, he covered rear. Reached tree line. RPGs fired at rear tank — eight hits no effect. Eventually hit commander’s hatch, smoke emerged. Crew likely wounded, driver reversed through column, reached regiment.
One hour since start. Fire dying down. I said, “Dima, let’s reach column end!” Under bridge, saw seven in Afghan hats, two corpses. Approached, one turned — black beard, hooked nose, wild eyes. I raised rifle… others were ours — contract soldier. A BMP crawled, collecting wounded. Hit in torsion, limping. Picked up wounded, moved to road; ammo vehicles burning around. Fighting almost ceased.
Closer to Argun, men shouted about wounded. Jumped to help, found major — camouflage, Marine insignia, bullet wounds. I applied a tourniquet. They brought beer, cigarettes, juice. Dark approached, militants descending, artillery carefully fired on slopes, helicopters hit mountains. Reinforcements arrived: T-72, BMP, another tank. Tank aimed at us — we waved, tank fired at greenery, 20 meters away. Troops from 324th Recon crawled, fired. I directed them, ordered ten men forward. Counted over forty burned bodies. Some vehicles untouched, showing militants had intel. Delayed support — came two and a half hours later, battle already over.

Bad idea to remind me. This is a painful topic for many. This was found in the diary of one of the fallen. Damn! If I get out, I’ll quit smoking, drinking, and that sort of thing — but later! Right now, I need to hold on! Where the hell is the BON! — Maljava, damn it, call reinforcements, they’ll tear us apart! — They told you ten times — not before an hour, unless they get burned on the way. — An hour from now we could be buried in a matchbox! — Well, ask the militants to stop shooting? — We need to retreat, the company commander is dead! BON is lost somewhere, the paratroopers are asleep too…
Fed up! A few more such conversations and I’d lose it myself. Need to focus, distract myself as best I can in this hell.
Then, as if on cue, fire from the militants ceased. Mines stopped whistling, ricochets died down, no “native” screams. Silence hung — worse than the mines’ whistling. — Did they run out of ammo? — Hell no, they’re plotting something, bastards.
Then gunfire roared from the opposite side. — What is it? — No idea! — Guys, that’s the paratroopers! Definitely elite! No one else! — Hooray! — Go get the bastards!
Without orders, everyone rose from the fortifications as one and rushed to the modest two-to-three story buildings. The militants quickly realized they were being attacked from two sides. Some tried to break out but were almost completely wiped out, the rest were finished off slowly. Clearing the buildings I remember vaguely (on the way to Khankala, slightly concussed).
Then our long-awaited BON and paratrooper units arrived. Much later, a mini-brigade of “commanders’ fathers” showed up and… a quiet (as much as possible in Chechnya) winter evening. Sitting on the steps of the new headquarters, smoking, thinking that it’s finally over. We are Internal Troops — so basically cops, and our job is to guard cops. Means the paratroopers will hit Grozny, I’ve had enough fighting! Six days later, our brigade was sent to Grozny…
Will you tell a joke?
Heh, it happens.

Listen. Male solidarity. One summer, my friend and I were sitting in a car, drinking beer. Windows open in nearby houses. Silence around, just our music. From the second floor came moans and groans, we turned down the music, listening, laughing. Suddenly the moans stopped and a girl shouted: — And that’s it? The guys didn’t even finish the song! My friend stuck his head out and yelled: — Don’t crush the guy, we paused it!

Listen. In university, a psychology lecture. Professor: — Today we’ll study three initial stages of human psyche: surprise, irritation, and anger. He dials a random number. — Hello, is Vasya there? — No, he doesn’t live here. — See, — the professor smiles, — that’s mild surprise. He dials again. — Hello, Vasya didn’t answer? — No such person here. The professor rubs his hands, winks at the class, dials a third time. — So no Vasya? — Go to hell! — Well, hope the example is clear. A student from the front row stands. — Excuse me, professor, but you forgot the fourth stage. — Which one? — The stage of utter disbelief. He approaches, dials. — Hello, this is Vasya. No one called me?

Listen. It was December 31, around 10 p.m. Going to celebrate New Year. Carrying three decent bags — you know what’s inside. Slightly drunk ourselves. Five minutes from the destination, anticipating fun, suddenly… a police car softly brakes about ten meters away. Sergeant gets out, heading straight for us. Thoughts — worst case, money extorted, New Year in the cell. Sergeant approaches: — Put the bags down! — we obey. I say to my friend: — Hands out. He spreads his hands. — A handful! — he places a handful in his hands. Sergeant reaches into his pocket, dumps a handful of candies into his hands, then turns to the car. From the car he calls back: — You’re expecting a New Year miracle, right!

Listen. Morning assembly. Company commander’s grim gaze inspects the formation. Stops on a recruit without a belt. — Comrade soldier, where is your belt? Soldier, clearly from an educated family but learning army lingo, tries to explain: — Comrade Major, it was stolen. — “Stolen” doesn’t exist in the army, — replies the commander. Soldier blushes from freckles to ears. Then commander: — In the army, the word is “lost it.”

Listen. After a battle, soldiers sit angry — many wounded, couldn’t drive Germans from heights. Discuss what to do if Hitler were caught. One: — Drown him in shit! Others: — No, too painful. Sergeant smokes. Second: — Quarter the bastard! All: — No, too fast. Sergeant smokes. Third: — Tie him in an anthill, let them eat him! — No, visibility bad. Sergeant finishes smoking: — Now listen, sons, what we’ll really do! Take an iron rail, heat one end, shove the other where it hurts! Grave silence. Youngest asks: — Sergeant, why cold? Should be the other way! Sergeant instructs: — So allies won’t pull him out!!!

Listen. Traveling in an open carriage: Emperor Alexander III, Prince Golitsyn, Lieutenant Rzhevsky — on guard. Suddenly a bomb thrown by a revolutionary. Rzhevsky intercepts, throws into Neva. Emperor: — Lieutenant, I owe you my life — ask anything! — I ask for Princess Golitsyn’s hand. Emperor to Golitsyn: — Prince, you hear? He hesitates: — Your Majesty… — Prince, I understand. General Rzhevsky asks for your daughter’s hand. — But we are one of Russia’s oldest families… — Fine, Prince, I agree. General Rzhevsky asks. — Oh Majesty! Others may object! — I accept all conditions. General Rzhevsky asks. Golitsyn: — I agree. Rzhevsky shoves him aside, plops next to Emperor, pats Tsar on the knee: — Sanya, why do we need this princess? Look at all the women around!

Listen. Young man in a cafe, coffee, notices a beautiful girl at the next table. Finally: — Can I join you? Girl shouts to the whole cafe: — I’m not sleeping with you! Everyone smiles. Young man blushes, returns to table. Girl joins him: — Sorry, I’m a psychologist writing a dissertation on “Behavior of different people in inadequate situations.” Young man, to whole cafe: — WHAT?! TWO HUNDRED BUCKS?! Girl runs outside. He calmly gathers himself, goes out, says: — Pleased to meet you, I’m a lawyer and I know how to make people feel guilty...

Listen. Scientists created a unique AI computer with vast knowledge, speech processor, and emotion synthesizer. It should answer any question. Before showing it to the public, they asked a tricky question. — Can a man screw a woman? — asks a creator. — Can, but hitting a woman is immoral, — answers the computer. — How stupid he is! — creators laugh. — I won’t answer anything else, — says the computer. — Fuck off.

Listen. A priest was building a church. All was fine, but the bell tower kept falling. Built again, fell again. Angel appeared: “Wall in your wife in the foundation and your church will last forever.” Priest loved his young wife, but God too. Needed the church. He sadly walled her in. Bell tower still fell. Because structural mechanics cannot be fooled.

Listen. Two cars at an intersection: a 600 and a Kopiyka. Big guy exits 600, chains in hand, same from Kopiyka. 600 driver: — You hit a new car, same model! Other: — You hit it! — calls on mobile. An hour later, they bring a brand new zero Mercedes. Other: — Got a new one in an hour?! First, stunned: — Yeah… — Now YOU get me a NEW KOPIYKA! In an hour! Time starts!

Well, listen. A guy tries to pick up a tipsy girl in a bar: "I bet 10 bucks I can impress you like never before without even touching you." "You’re an idiot, that’s impossible. Someone stop him!" They shake on it, he takes her home, and they spend the night chatting and playing games. In the morning, she wakes him and says: "That was really the best night of my life, but you kept joking around." "Take your ten bucks on the table," he mumbles in his sleep and turns to the wall.

Well, listen. A scary woman, with a face like a brick wall, enters a shopping center with her two sons, glaring at everyone and scolding the kids for the slightest thing. A shop assistant greets her: "Hello, welcome. What cute children! Twins?" "What nonsense are you talking, silly? One is 9, the other 8! Are you blind or just foolish?" "Neither, I just couldn’t believe someone dared to mess with you twice," she replies.

Well, listen. At a resort, an unremarkable little man kept pestering a gorgeous lady about having sex with him. She just laughed at him, saying, “With your talents...” Then the man bet her that he could have sex with her twenty times in a row. The only condition was that everything had to take place in complete darkness and that he had to wash himself after each time. The bet was a car, and the woman agreed. Night fell, and the man got down to business... Once, twice, three times... after the seventeenth time, the woman couldn't take it anymore and begged: “That's it! I lost! I can't take it anymore, turn on the light!” The light came on, and she saw a completely unfamiliar, burly man standing in front of her. “Who are you?!” she screamed. “What are you doing here? Where's the short, skinny one?” “Oh, you mean the entertainer? He sells tickets at the entrance...”

Well, listen. Attention! A new scam! Important to know! A group of young girls operates as follows: You leave the supermarket, approach your car, load your groceries, and get ready to leave. At that moment, two girls run up, asking for a ride to the subway, claiming their money was stolen and their mother is waiting. You agree. One sits in front, the other in back. Suddenly, while you’re distracted, they start creating chaos in the car. Questions many wonder: 1) Which supermarket should you watch? 2) What does the one in the back like to eat?

Well, listen. A schoolgirl is getting ready for prom and asks her mother: "Mom, if I kiss a boy, can I get pregnant?" Mother (smiling): "No, of course not." "What if we kiss more passionately?" Mother (tense): "Also no." "What if I do more intimate things?" Mother (shocked): "No, you won’t get pregnant." "And what about other experiments?" Mother (fainting): "God, daughter, are you planning all this at prom?!" "No, mom, don’t worry. I’m just figuring things out."

Well, listen. Grandpa, a WWII veteran, was sent to Germany on a tour by his children. At the embassy, he fills out a form: "Date of last visit to Germany." He writes: April–May 1945. "Mode of transport" (underline as appropriate)... options: plane, train, car… other. Grandpa skips everything and writes: "T-34 tank!"

Well, listen. A prosecutor reports to the president: "Vasilyeva pointed to Serdyukov, Serdyukov to Chubais, Chubais to Surkov, Surkov to Medvedev, Medvedev to..." "Enough," interrupts Vladimir Vladimirovich, "it’s already quite clear that nobody is guilty of anything..."

Well, listen. A clever insurance salesman: New soldiers were herded into a room. The insurer stepped onto the stage, smiled and said: "Guys, I know you don’t want to listen to nonsense, so I’ll explain insurance in one minute. Simple: if you don’t have it and die in battle, the government sends your family condolences. If you have it and die, the government sends your family $20,000." He paused, scanned the room, and asked: "So, who do you think will be sent to battle first?"

Well, listen. A husband and wife go to bed. Husband, half-asleep, says: "Svet..." Wife slaps him hard on the forehead; he pops his eyes open: "For what?" "What Svet? I’m Galya!" "What Galya?! You fool! Turn off the light!"

Well, listen. A man committed a crime. He was caught and brought before the king. The punishment was death, but the king offered him a choice: hanging or entering a large, black, steel door. The man chose hanging. As the noose was placed, he asked: "What’s behind that door?" The king laughed: "Funny thing, I offer this choice to everyone, and all choose the noose." "But what’s behind the door?" asked the man. "I won’t tell anyone," said the king. After a pause, he added: "Freedom. But people fear the unknown more than the rope!"
Tell a story about the Zone.
Interesting.

So we climbed into that hangar. Dust, cobwebs, the works—all the way to the roof; detectors went haywire, claiming there was no one around, not even us. We went to cover positions: Sanyok up front, the rest of us providing support… Then—CRASH! Huge rusty beams fell from the ceiling. Sanyok somehow dodged, but one beam landed exactly where he had been. He went down and started firing from the ground! The rest of us panicked and scattered. Sanyok started shouting about something in the corner. I looked—nothing there. It was chaos. We cautiously moved through the ruins for a few minutes, trying not to end up under another falling beam. Everyone started imagining things, firing at empty spaces. Two of the guys got unlucky: one fell into a hole in the floor, the other got hit by a falling door. We were sure some creature was messing with us. We activated psychic defense, threw gas grenades into the corners, and waited, guns ready. Turns out the creature was flying—it had been above the ceiling all along, having fun while we struggled below. Eventually, it brought down a few more beams onto us, flattening four guys. That’s when we got it. Sanyok heard rustling above and fired instinctively. We all joined in, hitting it however we could. Finally, the commander pulled us off before things got worse.

Have you heard of the Guide? The very first stalker. The first to venture into the Zone, and still alive. They say he can lead you anywhere. The problem is finding him—he doesn’t advertise, and he doesn’t take just anyone. He charges a lot, but if you find him and make a deal, he’ll take you exactly where you need to go. Now that’s someone worth talking to. He probably knows what’s behind the radar…

Showing off and having money are two very different things. Do you think money here is given away for free? No way. Without thinking, you’re either going to be eaten by some creature or killed by another stalker. You need to be careful. Smart people survive, fools don’t last long. Once, the three of us—me, Sinyak, and Tsepa—were cutting through a yard. We saw a small deer dragging a bag, unnoticed. Sinyak whispered to check it out, but I said, "Watch where he’s going first." Suddenly, it fell into a hole. While waiting, Tsepa quietly approached and grabbed some loot. When we started searching, the trap went off, and I had to retreat fast. Came back a month later, everything gone. Then I ventured inside myself and found a hidden hatch with some treasures. Lucky me, I didn’t come back empty-handed.

They say there’s a group of guys—some call them shamans, some druids. They don’t carry electronics, closer to nature. They claim the Zone reacts violently to electronics and emits dangers if disturbed. A proper person can handle the Zone without fancy equipment. They do carry weapons, though. Their survival rate is about the same as ours. Most operate from the Belarusian side. Would be interesting to watch them and see how many artifacts they carry.

Anyone heard of the secret labs deep in the Zone? Supposedly still operational. It’s unclear how anyone survives there without supplies or energy. Maybe it’s not humans working there. I spoke to a guy who never saw a lab but intercepted strange communications at Yantra. Someone mentioned a lab, and another swore he saw a figure in advanced gear, half-transparent, telling him to leave. He panicked. Could be mutations, or energy-based entities. Hard to know.

You know what I’ll say? In this part of the Zone, it’s almost impossible to sleep. Some people get nightmares so severe they don’t wake up. I’ve heard of a trick: find an old bucket or large pot, put your head inside, and sleep like nothing exists. Lately, soldiers have been wandering around in new helmets—apparently with a wire mesh inside. They’re preparing approaches, maybe towards the Sarcophagus. Best be far from here when that happens.

Hey, guys, have you heard about the Interceptors? They’re specialists, trained soldiers. They operate differently from border guards—stationed in the Zone for months, with multiple bases and supply drops. Commander is called Gromov—or “Major” or “Grom.” They can detect monsters from 200 meters away. They even have people who can tame creatures. Artifacts? Plenty. The Duty faction knows about them, but they stay quiet.

Brothers, have you heard about a few intruders near the antennas? Monolith guided us, and the brothers spotted them just in time. One was destroyed immediately. Two ran away. Cowards. Only true faith leaves no room for fear. Monolith is wise; he let them live for a reason.

Brothers, Monolith called me at night. I spoke with him. His presence was magnificent. I almost blinded myself, but his light filled me with strength. I know his power is with all of us. He said only strong faith can defeat evil. His power will support the faithful. Our victory is near!

I think… maybe this is all an experiment on humans. Maybe some little green men… Or maybe the Zone itself is a testing ground. Are they tracking how we react to artifacts? Or maybe it’s some sort of global experiment, scattered in labs for the rich to observe. Hard to know for sure… anyone who’s been near the radar can’t really say. Thoughts keep racing… It’s unsettling.

I was in the Dark Valley. Many unbelievers, as the Monolith’s power hasn’t reached there. We were guarding. At night, we were attacked. We fought fiercely. Monolith later gave the brothers a reward. For me, a trial. My weapon failed, but Monolith’s power guided me. Strong faith allowed me to defeat the enemies and come closer to him. I became a shadow, struck from behind, unseen. His power finished the rest. I am honored to serve, Perfect One!

My partner and I had been watching that research facility for a while. Very remote, no animals, no mutants. Anyone who went there returned terrified, or never at all. We decided to scout it ourselves. Reached the edge of the forest at dawn, I climbed a tree to observe. Buildings were mostly intact, quiet, no movement. Suddenly, gunfire nearby. On a roof, a strange man was frantically shooting. Then… the chaos began. The man’s weapon flew from his hands as if it had wings. He screamed, wielded a knife. Another identical figure appeared, floating in the air. For five minutes, he was cut and thrown around like a rag. I was drenched in sweat. No creatures appeared in normal or infrared vision. When we returned to base, I was exhausted, and everyone stared at me like I’d gone mad.

Brothers, we were patrolling the outskirts of the Black Forest and came across a lone unbeliever. He began to show signs of turning, so we didn’t kill him, though he was still half in his animal state. I know the wise Monolith allowed this for all of us: he showed the true face of his enemies once more. Our faith is stronger than ever! Victory over the unbelievers approaches, brothers! Monolith will fulfill our deepest desires. The truly devoted will be brought closer to him, to his glory and greatness. We will dwell forever in his radiance!

So my buddy told me this. He was woken up in the middle of the night by his superiors—rush to the lab, a shipment arrived. Half-asleep, he didn’t get it: what shipment? They said: “Have you been drinking government booze? There’s a fresh corpse on the table, dinner’s served!” He goes there, and what he sees… he’d seen lots of messed-up stalkers: torn, cooked, mutated—but here, something was in the stomach. At first glance, nearly human, but the head was a huge lumpy pumpkin, and the body was skin and bones. The mess was orange-red all over—blood, basically. Ammo had been used generously… searching for anomalies, you know. The body looked like a mix of a colander and a torn blanket, clearly played with by its “owners.” He spent the rest of the night and half the morning sorting it out. Apparently, this was the creature locals call the Illusionist: black cloths, visions for stalkers, a head nearly a meter around. The face looked like a tiny crown, eyes barely open, nose like a pin, huge mouth. It drags its head because it can’t hold it upright. And guess what it eats for dinner? Mushrooms! Yes, stalker mushrooms. And in a pouch on its side? Dried brains. And whose? Controller brains!

We had a fool join us… We went for a five-minute rest, and he immediately relaxed. But when moving, he lagged behind. Eventually, a Controller caught him—straight for the brains. Newcomers, remember this: every sound matters. Even a rabbit—though rabbits here are dangerous. Be ready every second: magazines and grenades at hand, gun always ready. See something? Fire half a mag immediately. Something behind a bush? Shoot first, ask later. That’s the rookie’s course: don’t get fancy or they’ll devour you. As for Controllers, a separate story. Shoot at a sound? Drop immediately. They latch onto you if you’re in sight—they need focus. Listen for movement, click the magazine quietly, prep grenades… Count to three and move far away. Stay alert—only that way you survive.

I wonder… maybe everything here is an experiment on humans? Maybe some little green men… not a worse theory than others. If this is a testing ground, what exactly are they trying? Which artifacts make us drool most? Or maybe it’s bombs? Scattered worldwide to labs for the rich… fancy that! I’d love to get my hands on that! Bought some secret stuff, paid a fortune, bragged about it, and… it blows up spectacularly! Honestly, who knows what’s real. Anyone who went near the radar can’t really tell. Maybe they know, maybe not—they just drool. Thoughts keep racing… unsettling, really.

Someone asked why only our eggheads mess with the Zone while foreigners stay away, despite mountains of Nobel-worthy material here. Here’s the answer. First, why bother personally when you can fund ours and get everything delivered on a plate? Second, foreigners have been here, but in the last two years they avoid it after one major disaster. They now buy data and materials, pay well, many lone operatives and looters work for them. The story? Thirty top scientists from around the world, heavily funded, armed military squad for protection, three guides from our side. Loaded on armored BTRs, set off. Returned a week later—twelve people on a wrecked BTR. Ambushed at Rotten Forest by looters. Only three survived—two ours, one foreigner. The Zone claimed the rest. Heard of Town-32? That’s where it happened. Initially calm, they collected samples and took measurements. Then madness struck—mass psychosis. People tore at themselves, hit heads on walls, or shot themselves. Meanwhile, beasts attacked. Survivors barely escaped, dodging anomalies. From then on, Town-32 remains off-limits, and foreigners won’t enter even for big money.

So, here’s what I wanted to say. When Duty captured that Illusionist, I was nearby—actually by accident. My Niva’s right front wheel gave out, bolts sheared. No spare parts. I swore, then thought I’d check the factory for replacements—about half an hour round trip. I’d been there before. Suddenly, I saw Duty patrol—fifty of them! I dove into the bushes. If noticed, I’d be dead. I lay low, listening. Gunfire erupted—never heard volleys like that except during the First Base capture. After five minutes, quiet. I peeked with binoculars—Duties were carrying their blinded men and the Illusionist on a tarp, just scraps left. They disappeared into a ravine. Curious, I went inside. Nothing useful after Duty—just two mags, a gun pinned under a beam. Bolts? Forgot all about them. I returned to my car, salvaged what I could, and drove off cautiously. Had I arrived half an hour earlier… wow…

Yes, I reached the outskirts of Pripyat. First thing, scout a high point—a big boiler with a chimney. Carefully entered, everything dusty, no signs of activity. Climbed inside the chimney via internal steps. Beautiful view, half the city visible! Binoculars out. About to descend, realized I hadn’t checked directly below—looked inside the courtyard: a dwarf wandering. Luckily, he didn’t see me. His AK was strapped on, I couldn’t aim well from the chimney. Using my Makarov was useless at that distance. I watched—he rummaged through debris, then froze. The pile started moving on its own! Dwarf jumped, covered his head, hands contorted. Debris flew from all directions, hitting him, yet he survived and walked away. I barely caught my breath. Suddenly smoke, fire below! I scrambled, used my forty-meter rep rope, secured it, wrapped a scarf, and descended, praying. Almost fell five meters short. Lucky, no major injuries, just scrapes and a pulled leg. Could’ve been worse.

I couldn’t believe my eyes. Beautiful girls exist, sure—but this one… perfection. The world around her seemed black-and-white; she alone in color and sharpness. Her movements! Never seen anything like it. My throat tightened. What’s she doing here in the Zone? Evening, near the bus station, a nine-story building. Something moved in a first-floor window. I drew my AK, cautious. She emerged. I stared, didn’t hear a word. Blue jumpsuit, snug, not loose—fantastic figure. Only then did I realize I wasn’t listening at all—just smiling like an idiot. She was from a research team, working on Zone endemics, escaped the guards to come alone. I agreed to help fetch some things. Arrived two days later, almost got caught several times while collecting. All I could think about: seeing her again. She radiated from within…
